entries.title: Bears transaction tracker: Who's coming, going this offseason
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> have been busy with a flurry of moves as they try to sharpen their roster — and make prudent salary cap decisions. </p><p>They can start agreeing to deals with free agents Monday, though players can't officially sign their contracts until the start of the league year Wednesday.</p><p>Here's a look at who the Bears have gained, and lost: </p><h3><b>March 8</b></h3><p>Defensive end <b>Daniel Hardy</b>, who has been a special teams ace, agreed to sign a two-year deal worth as much as $6 million to return to the Bears, per NFL Network. Hardy played only 5% of the Bears' defensive snaps last year but appeared in a whopping 78% of their kicking plays, totaling 22 tackles.</p><h3><b>March 6</b></h3><p>The day <b>Drew Dalman</b> put in his retirement paperwork, the Bears traded a 2027 fifth-round draft pick to the Patriots for his replacement, center <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/06/bears-trade-garrett-bradbury-patriots-center-2027-fifth-round-pick-nfl" target="_blank" ><b>Garrett Bradbury</b></a>. Bradbury, who started every game for the AFC champions last year, has one year left on a $9.6 million contract he signed last year after spending six years with the Vikings.</p><h3><b>March 5</b></h3><p>Needing to clear cap space and cognizant of their depth at wide receiver, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/halas-intrigue/2026/03/05/halas-intrigue-podcast-breaking-down-bears-dj-moore-trade-nfl" target="_blank" >the Bears traded</a> <b>DJ Moore</b> and a fifth-round pick to the Bills for a second-round draft pick this year. Moore jad just 682 receiving yards on 50 catches last year, the worst numbers of his career. Had the Bears kept Moore, he would have cost a $24.5 million salary cap hit, the third-highest on the team.</p><p>Eight days after telling linebacker <b>Tremaine Edmunds</b> and his agent that they could seek a trade, the Bears told Edmunds <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/05/bears-release-tremaine-edmunds-linebacker-salary-cap-nfl" target="_blank" >they were cutting him</a>. They saved $15 million in cap space in the process. Edmunds had four interceptions in 13 games last year, the second-most among all NFL linebackers.</p><h3><b>March 3</b></h3><p>Center <b>Drew Dalman</b>, who played every snap and reached the Pro Bowl in his first year with the Bears, told the team he planned to retire after just five years in the NFL. He filed his retirement paperwork three days later.</p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Man in vehicle found fatally shot in Austin
entries.summary: <p>A man was found fatally shot in a vehicle Sunday night in Austin on the West Side. </p><p>Around 7:50 p.m., the 31-year-old man was found shot inside the vehicle in the 4700 block of West Arthington Street, Chicago police said. </p><p>He was taken to Mount Sinai Hospital with a gunshot wound to his chest and was pronounced dead, police said. </p><p>Area 4 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: ‘Country’ Joe McDonald, ‘60s rock musician, Woodstock standout, dies at 84
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — “Country” Joe McDonald, a hippie rock star of the 1960s whose “I-Feel-Like-I’m-Fixin’-To-Die Rag” was a four-lettered rebuke to the Vietnam War that became an anthem for protesters and a highlight of the Woodstock <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music" >music</a> festival, died Sunday. He was 84.</p><p>McDonald, who performed with his band, Country Joe and the Fish, died in Berkeley, California. His <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/obituaries" >death</a> from complications of Parkinson’s disease was reported by Kathy McDonald, his wife of 43 years, in a statement issued by his publicist.</p><p>McDonald was a longtime presence in the Bay Area music scene, where peers included the Grateful Dead, the Jefferson Airplane and his onetime girlfriend, Janis Joplin. He wrote or co-wrote hundreds of songs, from psychedelic jams to soul-influenced rockers, and released dozens of albums. But he was known best for a talking blues he completed in less than an hour in 1965 — the year President Lyndon Johnson began sending ground forces to Vietnam — and recorded in the Berkeley home of Arhoolie Records founder Chris Strachwitz.</p><p>In the deadpan style of McDonald’s hero, Woody Guthrie, “I-Feel-Like-I’m-Fixin’-To-Die Rag” was a mock celebration of war and early, senseless death, with a chorus concertgoers and others would learn by heart:</p><p>And its 1, 2, 3 what are we fighting for? Don’t ask me I don’t give a damn, Next stop is Vietnam, And its 5, 6, 7 open up the pearly gates, Well there ain’t no time to wonder why, WHOOPEE we’re all gonna die</p><p>At the time he wrote “I-Feel-Like-I’m-Fixin’-To-Die Rag,” McDonald was co-leader of the newly formed Country Joe and the Fish and he added a special “F-I-S-H” chant before the song: “Give me an F, give me an I, give me an S, give me an H.” By the time his group appeared at Woodstock in 1969, the Fish were on the verge of breaking up, the chant was a different four-letter word beginning in “F” and McDonald was performing before hundreds of thousands. Many would stand and sing along, a moment captured in the Woodstock documentary released the following year. (For the film, the song’s lyrics appeared as subtitles, a bouncing ball on top).</p><p>“Some people alluded to peace and stuff [at Woodstock], but I was talking about Vietnam,” McDonald told the Associated Press in 2019. He called the opening chant “an expression of our anger and frustration over the Vietnam War, which was killing us, literally killing us.”</p><p>The song helped make him famous, but brought legal and professional consequences. In 1968, Ed Sullivan canceled a planned appearance by Country Joe and the Fish on his variety show when he learned of the new opening cheer. Soon after Woodstock, McDonald was arrested and fined for using the cheer at a show in Worcester, Massachusetts, an ordeal which helped hasten the band’s demise.</p><p>McDonald even performed the song in court. His friendships with such political radicals as Abbie Hoffman and Jerry Rubin led to his being called in as a witness in the “Chicago Eight (or Seven)” trial against organizers of anti-war protests at the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2024-democratic-national-convention/2024/08/09/democratic-national-convention-chicago-1968-protests-vietnam-grant-park-chicago-police-department" >1968 Democratic National Convention in Chicago</a>. On the stand, he explained how he had met with Hoffman and others and told them about “I-Feel-Like-I’m-Fixin’-To-Die Rag.” When he began performing it, the judge interrupted and told him “No singing is permitted in the courtroom.”</p><p>McDonald recited the words instead.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/02/25/buddy-guy-sinners-movie-ryan-coogler-blues-music" >Buddy Guy’s screen time in ‘Sinners’ is brief, but his legacy runs deep</a> </li> </ul> </div> <div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/03/01/review-illinois-native-margo-price-hard-headed-woman" >Review: Outlaw country artist Margo Price pulls no punches in 90-minute showdown at Metro</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Driver wounded in Dan Ryan Expressway shooting on the South Side
entries.summary: <p>A driver was wounded in a shooting on the Dan Ryan Expressway Sunday night on the South Side. </p><p>State troopers responded to the outbound lanes of the Dan Ryan near 18th Street around 6:30 p.m. for the shooting, according to Illinois state police. </p><p>One of the vehicles crashed into a light pole off the interstate at 36th Street and Wentworth Avenue and the driver was taken to an area hospital with injuries from a gunshot wound, state police said. His condition wasn’t immediately known. </p><p>The roadway is expected to be shut down for canvassing, officials said. </p><p>Anyone with information on the shooting is encouraged to contact Illinois State Police Zone 1 at 847-294-4400 or email ISP.CrimeTips@Illinois.gov.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Oliver Moore's injury further weakens Blackhawks' center corps in loss to Stars
entries.summary: <p>DALLAS — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> rookie Sam Rinzel had just begun celebrating his third career NHL goal Sunday when he realized only three teammates were in the huddle, not four.</p><p>Seconds before the goal, fellow rookie Oliver Moore appeared to injure his right knee on an awkward crash into the end-boards. The celebration stopped so Rinzel could help Moore hobble off the ice and down to the locker room.</p><p>Moore’s injury further weakened the Hawks’ already-slim group of centers and took the wind out of the team’s sails. The early two-goal lead Rinzel gave the Hawks eventually slipped away in a 4-3 overtime loss to the Stars.</p><p>After the game, coach Jeff Blashill said Moore won’t play Monday against the Mammoth but didn’t offer a more detailed update.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">GOAL: Sam Rinzel rips a shot from the slot for his 3rd goal of the season, though Oliver Moore was shaken up on the play. <a href="https://t.co/0dVFNqKMHH">pic.twitter.com/0dVFNqKMHH</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2030771754939961442?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 8, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>All four of the Stars' goals were scored around the net in scrappy, ugly fashion. It wasn’t a pretty game in general.</p><p>Hawks goalie Arvid Soderblom, making his second consecutive start because of Spencer Knight’s illness, made some difficult saves but probably should have made a few more straightforward ones, including on Stars defenseman Miro Heiskanen’s tip-in winner 22 seconds into overtime.</p><p></p><p>‘‘I would just say you’re going to give that [shot] up, and it went in,’’ Blashill said.</p><p></p><p>The Hawks earned a point after Connor Bedard, who was stymied for most of the game, scored from Fort Worth — 54 feet out, technically — with 1:20 left in regulation. It was the Hawks’ first goal with their goalie pulled all season. They had been the only team without one.</p><p></p><p>But nobody reasonably could argue they deserved the full two points. The Stars finished with a 32-17 advantage in shots on goal after holding the Hawks without one for nearly the whole second period.</p><p></p><p>‘‘At times, the ice tilted their way on us, so then you’re defending a little bit more than you want to,’’ Blashill said. ‘‘But I also thought we passed up on some shots. We’ve got to make sure we have a good shooting mentality.’’</p><p></p><p>The Hawks’ lack of center depth after trading Jason Dickinson and Nick Foligno might become a really significant problem without Moore. They have gone an atrocious 31-for-97 at the faceoff dot in their last two games, for example.</p><p></p><p>They did call up and play rookie forward Nick Lardis on Sunday, but he’s not a center and barely exceeded 10 minutes of ice time even in an 11-forward rotation.</p><p></p><p>‘‘Coming back here now, it feels a lot less stressful,’’ Lardis said. ‘‘[I want to] just show that I can be trusted by the coaches more. It goes for every young player starting their pro career, but [it’s about] being trusted as a two-way player and continuing to show my consistency and my strengths.’’</p><p></p><p>Then again, the Hawks’ inexperience at every position group <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/06/blackhawks-trade-deadline-reaction-run-by-kids-kyle-davidson-connor-bedard" target="_blank" >is intentional</a>. It’s a feature, not a bug. It might be a disadvantage in the short term, but management hopes giving so many young guys so much responsibility will pay off down the road.</p><p></p><p>There’s no denying the fact the youngsters rapidly are accumulating experience in close-game scenarios. Discounting empty-netters, five of the Hawks’ six games since the Olympic break have been decided by one goal. The only exception was their blowout road victory against the Mammoth.</p><p></p><p>The catch is, the Hawks have lost all five of those one-goal games.</p><p></p><p>‘‘It’s not the most comfortable thing always, but you learn from experience,’’ said defenseman Matt Grzelcyk, now the Hawks’ oldest player at 32.</p><p></p><p>‘‘Hopefully [the kids] can take these next 20 games to heart and build toward the</p><p>future, for next season, [and] not just wait until the offseason to do that.’’</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Big Ten tournament preview: An Illinois-Michigan rematch Saturday at the UC would be huge
entries.summary: <p>The 29th <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >Big Ten men’s basketball</a> tournament starts Tuesday and ends Sunday at the United Center, where the champion will snip down the nets while the NCAA Tournament bracket reveal furiously gets underway.</p><p>In keeping with tradition, the NCAA selection committee will claim to have factored the Big Ten final into its seeding even though it — how to put this? — did not.</p><p>Unlike last year, when 15 teams made the Big Ten field, this tourney will include all 18 teams and begin a day sooner, because clearly a five-day event just wasn’t long enough.</p><p>A team-by-team glimpse, in order of seeding (odds via FanDuel):</p><p><b>1. MICHIGAN (29-2, 19-1)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>TBD, 11 a.m. Friday, BTN.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+105.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>If the ginormous frontcourt of Yaxel Lendeborg, Aday Mara and Morez Johnson Jr. brutalizes foes like it did Illinois in Champaign, it’s over-and-out for everybody else.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>Point guard Elliot Cadeau might wear down with injured L.J. Cason unable to spell him.</p><p><b>2. NEBRASKA (25-5, 15-5)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>TBD, 5:30 p.m. Friday, BTN.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+1000.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>No team plays harder at the defensive end, and the Huskers move the ball beautifully on offense. Is it shooter Pryce Sandfort’s time to shine?</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>Is coach Fred Hoiberg allowed to win a championship at the UC? Asking for a few million friends.</p><p><b>3. MICHIGAN STATE (25-6, 15-5)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>TBD, approximately 8 p.m. Friday, BTN.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+650.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>Jeremy Fears is one of the best playmakers around, and we are bound by law to say it’s hard to beat Tom Izzo in March.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>As tough as this team is, it lacks the kind of star power we’ve often seen in green.</p><p><b>4. ILLINOIS (24-7, 15-5)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>TBD, approximately 1:30 p.m. Friday, BTN.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+470.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>If freshmen Keaton Wagler and David Mirkovic hold up to postseason competition, the shooters around them will give the Illini a shot to win it for the third time in the 2020s.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>The Illini likely would have to get past Michigan in the semis, and the first meeting was ugly.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-830000" </div> </div><p><b>5. WISCONSIN (22-9, 14-6)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>Washington-USC winner, approximately 1:30 p.m. Thursday, BTN.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+3500.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>The team that popped Purdue for 97 points to ruin the Boilermakers’ Senior Day can beat anybody.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>The team that ham-fisted its way through recent lopsided losses at Ohio State and Oregon isn’t going anywhere.</p><p><b>6. UCLA (21-10, 13-7)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>Minnesota-Rutgers winner, approximately 8 p.m. Thursday, BTN.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+3500.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>When point guard Donovan Dent is on his game, this offense — particularly shooter Tyler Bilodeau — is dangerous.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>The Bruins are a different team, and not in a good way, away from home.</p><p><b>7. PURDUE (23-8, 13-7)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>TBD, 5:30 p.m. Thursday, BTN.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+550.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>Braden Smith, Trey Kaufman-Renn and Fletcher Loyer have been there so many times before.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>Who would’ve believed the Boilers would tumble all the way to seventh? Something’s off with Matt Painter’s crew.</p><p><b>8. OHIO STATE (20-11, 12-8)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>TBD, 11 a.m. Thursday, BTN.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+7500.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>Bruce Thornton, the school’s career scoring leader, isn’t the only Buckeye who can fill it up.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>Statistically, this team is right down the middle in the conference. That has “also-ran” written all over it.</p><p><b>9. IOWA (20-11, 10-10)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>Oregon-Maryland winner, 11 a.m. Wednesday, Peacock.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+5000.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>Guard Bennett Stirtz is terrific, and first-year coach Ben McCollum’s postseason record (most of it at Northwest Missouri State) is unreal.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>Stirtz doesn’t have a whole lot in the way of sidekicks.</p><p><b>10. INDIANA (18-13, 9-11)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>Northwestern-Penn State winner, 5:30 p.m. Wednesday, BTN.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+10000.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>Guard Lamar Wilkerson led the Big Ten in scoring in league play and had multiple 40-plus-point games.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>Look, it’s a football school and the whole world knows it.</p><p><b>11. MINNESOTA (15-16, 8-12)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>Rutgers, approximately 8 p.m. Wednesday, BTN.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+30000.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>The Gophers have beaten three higher seeds, one of them Michigan State.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>Minnesota still hasn’t won this tournament. Why start now?</p><p><b>12. WASHINGTON (15-16, 7-13)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>USC, approximately 1:30 p.m. Wednesday, Peacock.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+20000.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>The Huskies went 3-3 down the stretch and had late leads in two of the losses.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>One NCAA win in the last 14 years kind of says it all.</p><p><b>13. USC (18-13, 7-13)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>Washington, approximately 1:30 p.m. Wednesday, Peacock.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>+30000.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>A 4-1 stretch that began at Wisconsin looked really good.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>There have been seven straight losses since then, and star Chad Baker-Mazara was just booted from the program.</p><p><b>14. RUTGERS (13-18, 6-14)</b></p><p><b>Next: </b>Minnesota, approximately 8 p.m. Wednesday, BTN.</p><p><b>Title odds: </b>N/A.</p><p><b>Snip? </b>A lot of steals and not many turnovers from this squad.</p><p><b>Or slip? </b>Every league win came against Penn State, Maryland, Oregon or Northwestern.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: All the Kings men can't put former Bulls Zach and DeMar together again
entries.summary: <p>SACRAMENTO, Calif. – DeMar DeRozan and Zach LaVine as Bulls had some moments, even sniffed a playoff series, but toiled away far too often in mediocrity.</p><p>DeRozan and LaVine as Kings?</p><p>An unmitigated disaster.</p><p>Even before LaVine was shut down for the season for finger surgery and DeRozan was out on Sunday with illness, the one-two punch they showed under Billy Donovan did not translate to the move west. Obviously, they’ve had much different personnel around them in Sacramento, but also one that wasn’t void of talent when the roster was whole. Still, the synergy has evaporated.</p><p>“Sometimes it’s hard on the West Coast to watch all these guys play,” Donovan said, when asked what has changed with the two since they left. “They have had some injuries, and certainly their team here is a lot different with Zach and DeMar here than when we had them. The groups are different.</p><p>“Listen, I love both those two guys. I think those guys know how I feel about them. They were totally professional, and listen when you’re dealing with high-level players like that you are not always going to see eye-to-eye on stuff, but I appreciated the conversation and the intent on their part was to really try and win. That was important to those two guys. I don’t know all the reasons it didn’t work out (here).”</p><p>A question the Kings still haven’t answered.</p><p>Before LaVine was lost for the season, the last-place Kings were 9-30 when he played this year. They were 14-50 when DeRozan’s played. DeRozan has been with the Kings for two years, while LaVine was traded there last February.</p><p>It wasn’t like they were holding up Larry O’Brien Trophies with the Bulls, but they had more good moments together than bad. That included the first year they were teamed together, posting a 46-36 record, and had a first-place team until Lonzo Ball was lost for the season.</p><p>“It was a lot early on and I’m adding Vooch (Nikola Vucevic) in there, when they first got (to the Bulls) because they were all used to being the primary guy and they all got injected into each other,” Donovan said. “The one guy that had to make the biggest sacrifice to his offense was Vooch because he needed those guys, Zach and DeMar, to play with the ball in their hands.</p><p>“But I think their commitment, who they are as people, their professionalism, I loved being around both of them.”</p><p>DeRozan has one more year left on his deal, while LaVine has a player option for $48.9 million that he has already said he would pick up.</p><p></p><p><b>Back whole</b></p><p></p><p>As expected, the injury report for the Bulls looked a lot cleaner against Sacramento, as Josh Giddey (ankle), Matas Buzelis (ankle), Jalen Smith (calf) and Patrick Williams (quad) were all good to go. There were minutes restrictions on Smith and Williams because of the time they missed.</p><p>Giddey and Buzelis were back in the starting lineup, while Smith and Williams came off the bench.</p><p></p><p><b>Different Russ</b></p><p></p><p>Russell Westbrook was back in the limelight the last week after he called out the Kings media for being too negative.</p><p>Donovan coached Westbrook for years and had only great things to say about the veteran guard.</p><p>“I’ve always said this about him: It’s a great luxury as a coach when you walk into a locker room before the game and you never have to worry about a guy being shot out of a cannon,” Donovan said. “Like that guy comes ready to play every single night. Winning is a major priority for him. He plays with a fire and passion and plays hard every night.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: U.S. citizen from Skokie stopped at O'Hare, detained by immigration officials for nearly 30 hours
entries.summary: <p>A U.S. citizen from Skokie spent nearly 30 hours in custody by federal <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration" >immigration</a> officials after she was detained at O'Hare Airport following a work trip Thursday, drawing rebukes from family, friends and politicians. </p><p>Sundas "Sunny" Naqvi and five colleagues, including two other U.S. citizens and three individuals with green cards, were all detained at the airport by federal immigration agents for their "recent travel history" after returning from Istanbul, Cook County commissioner and family friend Kevin Morrison told reporters outside the Immigration and Customs Enforcement center in Broadview Sunday. </p><p>Naqvi, 28, and the others were taken to the Broadview facility where they were then transferred to a facility in Dodge County, Wisconsin, sometime Friday evening, according to Morrison. She was then released around 5 a.m. Saturday after nearly 30 hours and a woman gave her a ride to a hotel where she reunited with family who followed her phone's location until service died. </p><p>"All she was told was that there was some curious travel history, but they had no cause to detain her for those 30 hours," Morrison said, adding that federal agents "lied to" local law enforcement and elected officials by saying Naqvi was never detained. </p><p>Immigration officials didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.</p><p>"We need transparency, we need accountability and we need to make sure that we are incredibly mindful of the steps we must take as a nation to protect all of our civil rights and civil liberties," said Morrison. "The fact that this could happen to any U.S. citizen should terrify us all." </p><p>Naqvi's sister, Sarah Afzal, said she almost filed a missing persons report when she couldn’t get answers from local or federal police. </p><p>"She was right here and then she was in Wisconsin," Afzal said. "It was terrifying." </p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/08/deportation-francisco-gonzalez-jasso-panchito-gang-latin-kings-deported-ice-mexico-midway-blitz-little-village" >Labeled a gang member and deported, longtime Chicagoan tries to restart in his Mexican hometown</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>A number of candidates running for Congress were on hand at Broadview to talk to news media, including 7th District candidate Reed Showalter and 9th District candidates Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss, Kat Abughazaleh, Bushra Amiwala and Illinois state Sen. Laura Fine.</p><p>"They were only brought home after their families and our community refused to back down and kept asking for answers," Abughazaleh said. "This is going to be the new normal if we don't stand up against it." </p><p>Fine called the arrests "completely unconscionable" and emphasized the importance of continued congressional oversight. </p><p>"We are not getting the full story, we are not getting all of the facts and it is up to Congress to dig deep into what's going on so we understand as citizens and as residents what is happening and [so] that we could get down to the truth," Fine said. </p><p>Amiwala, who knows Naqvi, praised the efforts by family and friends to seek the woman's release.</p><p>"For her to have a community that she does — that was able to rally this many people on such short span — is a testament and reflection to her and her family," Amiwala said. </p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/27/chicago-ice-trump-bovino-marimar-martinez-crime-border-patrol-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >ICE protesters keep beating Trump in Chicago court, but the battles take a toll — ‘It’s about intimidation’</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/19/chicago-ice-crime-border-patrol-trump-midway-blitz-alex-pretti-renee-good-eileen-oneill-burke" target="_blank" >Cook County’s top prosecutor outlines plan to charge ICE agents with crimes</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/12/chicago-area-resident-lawsuit-facebook-ice-tracking-app" target="_blank" >Chicago-area resident sues Trump officials over Facebook shutting down popular ICE sighting page</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: Kyle Williams' Super 25 high school girls basketball rankings for March 8, 2026
entries.summary: <p>Nazareth left no doubt it was the best team in Illinois after it won its second state championship in four years with a dominant 55-23 victory Saturday against Loyola in the Class 4A title game in Normal. The Roadrunners ended the Ramblers’ 25-game winning streak and posted the largest margin of victory in a championship game since 2008.</p><p>Nazareth ended its season on a 25-game winning streak. Its only in-state loss was to Benet in December, and it faced a tough road to get to the state finals. The Roadrunners had to beat Young, St. Ignatius, Downers Grove North and Waubonsie Valley just to advance to the 4A semifinals.</p><p>But those battles prepared Nazareth for the two biggest games of its season, and it showed. The Roadrunners moved the ball seamlessly offensively and were active defensively, cutting off drives and limiting Loyola’s potent post scorers.</p><p>‘‘I’m super-excited we got to come back and prove ourselves and prove that we can hang with the best teams,’’ said Nazareth senior guard Lyla Shelton, who scored eight points.</p><p>Shelton, who is committed to Concordia University Wisconsin, was a steady contributor for the Roadrunners all season. She scored 18 points in their supersectional victory against Waubonsie Valley, brought constant energy and was always vocal.</p><p>I’ve thought Shelton has been underrated all season. She’s a reliable shooter who can play on and off the ball.</p><h3>‘One of the all-time greats’</h3><p>In the afterglow of Nazareth’s victory, coach Eddie Stritzel had high praise for freshman standout Mia Gage, who scored 11 points, grabbed five rebounds and had five steals in the title game.</p><p>‘‘Our guards put so much pressure on the ball, and it allows our wings to be really aggressive,’’ Stritzel said. ‘‘I think Mia is going to be one of the all-time greats.’’</p><p>Gage is usually the most athletic player on the court. She regularly uses her athleticism to jump into passing lines and grab rebounds, and she showcased some impressive shooting against Loyola.</p><p>Gage is now a state champion after an impressive freshman season. She credited her teammates for helping her acclimate to varsity basketball.</p><p>‘‘Knowing that my teammates are going to be there for the next pass or, if I get beat, then they’ll be there to help me,’’ Gage said.</p><h3>Runner-up Ramblers</h3><p>Despite the lopsided score in the title game, Loyola still had an impressive season.</p><p>The Ramblers lost senior guard Aubrey Galvan, who had shouldered a lot of the offensive responsibility, to graduation in 2025 but returned to Normal by doubling down on their defense and playing more through post players Maddison Locke and Emily Naraky.</p><p>Nazareth shut down the paint and prevented Locke and Naraky from getting easy looks. Loyola struggled to score, especially from three-point range (1-for-16).</p><p>Still, with Locke and Naraky returning, expect the Ramblers to be formidable again next season.</p><p><b>1. Nazareth (35-3) 1</b></p><p>Won the 4A state championship</p><p><b>2. Loyola Academy (34-3) 2</b></p><p>4A state runner-up</p><p><b>3. Waubonsie Valley (32-4) 3</b></p><p>Lost in the 4A super-sectional</p><p><b>4. Rolling Meadows (22-12) 8</b></p><p>Finished fourth in 4A</p><p><b>5. entries.title: Horoscope for Monday, March 09, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>Avoid shopping or important decisions from 6 a.m. until 11 a.m. After that, the moon moves from Scorpio into Sagittarius.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today has strong conciliatory vibes, which means it's the perfect day to patch up quarrels or reach out and smooth troubled waters. An apology or a gesture of affection will go a long way. Meanwhile, you might do something to improve your appearance or the image you project to your world.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Financial discussions will go well today. In fact, assistance from others or practical or financial support from someone might secretly benefit you. Some of you might also begin a secret flirtation or a romance with someone in a position of power. (An interesting day.)</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is an excellent day to reach out to close friends, partners and spouses to improve your connection with them. In fact, it's an especially good day if you need to extend an olive branch to someone or make amends for an argument or a hurtful comment.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You make a great impression on others today. In fact, this great impression could lead to a new romance with someone — a boss or someone in authority, probably work-related. Today people see you as sympathetic, caring and willing to help — which you are.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Grab every chance to travel for pleasure because this will delight you. Meanwhile, romance with someone from afar or from another culture might percolate in person or online. If so, this connection will be powerful. You might feel it is written in the stars.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a friendly, easygoing day for many (including you) because it's the perfect day to patch up family arguments or any dissension taking place at home. In particular, financial matters can be settled in a friendly way.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Relations with relatives, siblings and neighbors are particularly warm and friendly today. In fact, you might smooth troubled waters if necessary. This is also a passionate, romantic day for some of you. If so, this romance will feel fated — as if it were meant to be.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Issues related to cash flow, spending and expenses, your job, and your health will flow smoothly today. People will be helpful. In fact, for some of you, a work-related romance might begin. Relations with a family member will be meaningful in a powerful way.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a marvelous day for your sign because the moon is in Sagittarius, dancing beautifully with Venus, Saturn and Neptune, which will pave the way for loving, sympathetic and understanding relations with everyone. Romance can blossom! The arts are also blessed.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Transportation delays, mistakes and mixed-up communications continue to plague your days. However, this is an almost perfect day to patch up difficulties with family members. Feelings of mutual sympathy and understanding will give everyone a warm feeling in their stomach.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This might be an unexpectedly productive day for you if you have a chance to sit down with a friend or a member of a group and think about your hopes and dreams for the future. People are friendly and supportive today. Why not risk sharing your dreams with someone to help you define your goals?</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You're high visibility today. People notice you. Some might ask for your financial advice, while others might admire you because you appear to have values they respect. This is also a romantic day full of promise. You can't go wrong.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actor Oscar Isaac (1979) shares your birthday today. You are individualistic, enthusiastic, and imaginative. You want harmony in life and value justice. This is a year of work and construction (externally and internally) because it's time to build solid foundations. Simplicity is the key to life this year. Take charge of your health. Physical exercise will be important.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Bulls forget the running shoes for a night and Kings make them pay
entries.summary: <p>SACRAMENTO, Calif. — While the Bulls’ offense has slowed down a tick since the roster purge, it still would get ticketed in most school zones.It remains in the top three in pace.</p><p>But coach Billy Donovan had a message Sunday: Play faster. A message they get loud and clear but are still trying to work through as shown in a lackluster 126-110 loss to the lowly Kings.</p><p>“I knew a little bit about Coach [before the trades], and whenever we played the Bulls, we knew it was going to be a track meet, so bring your running shoes,” guard Collin Sexton said. “Now being on the team, it’s pretty cool to be in that position, to be in open space and getting used to it. That’s the biggest thing, trying to get used to it.”</p><p>Hence, the inconsistency.</p><p>The two-point victory Thursday against the Suns was the best Donovan’s team has looked since the roster was flipped at the trade deadline. The Bulls ran the Suns out of their building, beating them 22-10 on fast-break points, and had only 12 turnovers.</p><p>Even with a few practices under their belt since that showing, the game Sunday against the Kings was a reminder of how work they still has to be done in the running department. The Bulls only mustered four fast-break points in the first half and nine for the game.</p><p>They did get a team-high 28 points from Sexton, but he also left the game late with a leg contusion. Matas Buzelis added 20.</p><p>“I give the new guys grace because it is hard, especially when you add in the fast pace with which we play,” forward Isaac Okoro said of the inconsistencies in the high-tempo offense. “Guys are still getting used to that. It’s hard to go from a slower team to how we play, then also try to have that physical identity.”</p><p>They didn’t show either early against the Kings.</p><p>And while Okoro was spot on by pointing out that Anfernee Simons and Sexton came from systems that weren’t even close to running at the tempo the Bulls do, there’s also another layer to the ongoing issues. Some of the new additions weren’t even playing much, never mind conditioning.</p><p>“The biggest issue I think was the fact that a lot of those guys were coming from situations where they were not playing at all,” Donovan said. “Like [Yabusele Guerschon] wasn’t playing, Nick [Richards] wasn’t playing, Rob [Dillingham] wasn’t playing, so I think the pace has been a little different. We went through some of that with Tre [Young], Zach [Collins] and Kevin Huerter last year.”</p><p>That’s true, but Young and Huerter had games that easily translated to what Donovan was looking for. Dillingham is kind of in that same category because of his quickness, but for Richards, and especially Guerschon, this change of zip codes has been a wake-up call.</p><p>“I think they are getting better conditioned,” Donovan said. “I think the other part for us, which we’ve been trying to concentrate on and focus on, is when we get into the halfcourt, our spacing, guys understanding what we’re trying to do, how we’re trying to play.”</p><p>But who exactly will even be sticking around for that growth?</p><p>That’s the other layer to this. Donovan has been putting a lot of his time into speeding the new guys up, and more than half of them could be elsewhere next season because of free agency.</p><p>Until Donovan is told otherwise, however, it’s business as usual. Like Sexton said, bring the running shoes.</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Why Blackhawks GM Kyle Davidson rarely waits for deadline day to make trades
entries.summary: <p>DALLAS — Blackhawks general manager <b>Kyle Davidson</b> has established a preference for making moves well before the NHL trade deadline, rather than waiting until the last minute.</p><p>He dealt <b>Brandon Hagel</b> three days before the 2022 deadline, dealt <b>Patrick Kane </b>three days before the 2023 deadline and dealt <b>Seth Jones</b> six days before the 2025 deadline.</p><p>All were blockbuster trades with basically only one possible partner, so it seemed logical they would be consummated as soon as they were agreed upon.</p><p>But this year, even when shopping two regular rental players with plenty of suitors, the pattern continued. Davidson again acted quickly, dealing <b>Connor Murphy</b> on Monday and <b>Jason Dickinson</b> on Wednesday before the deadline Friday.</p><p>Why? He gave an interesting explanation Friday.</p><p>‘‘With Murphy and [Dickinson], I wanted to give them an opportunity,’’ Davidson said. ‘‘The last thing I would want is to hold out for a little something more, like this little bit more, and it dry up and they don’t get to experience [the playoffs]. Not that they asked to go out or anything. But in exploring the market, this was a great opportunity for both of them.</p><p>‘‘I’m not big on waiting to wait. If that sets the market, that sets the market, for better or worse. I would rather get a deal done with a return that I’m satisfied with ahead of time than sweat it out. It either goes sideways and you lose something, or you just wait for the sake of waiting.’’</p><p>Critics of Davidson pointed out that while he got a second-round draft pick for Murphy, the Canucks got second- and fourth-round picks two days later for a similar defenseman in <b>Tyler Myers</b>.</p><p>However, the Flyers suffered the opposite fate. They held out for the best offer for another similar defenseman in <b>Rasmus Ristolainen</b> and ended up not trading him (and therefore getting nothing for him).</p><p>It will be interesting to see whether Davidson continues to act early when/if he becomes a buyer in future years.</p><h3>Levshunov stabilizing</h3><p>When asked about defenseman <b>Artyom Levshunov’s</b> up-and-down season so far, it’s not surprising Davidson supported and praised him.</p><p>‘‘When you do something like we did with [him], where we took him out for a number of games — almost had a pseudo-training camp with him — that can go one of two ways,’’ Davidson said. ‘‘[He] dug in. He went full throttle into the development opportunity that was presented. [He did] everything we asked of him.’’</p><p>The individual practice program at least has stabilized Levshunov’s season. He still is making some mistakes, but they’re not as frequent or obvious as they were in late January.</p><p>The Hawks have outscored opponents 3-2 during his five-on-five ice time since the Olympic break. He has also taken on penalty-killing duties for the first time now that Murphy is gone.</p><p>‘‘‘He just looks a lot more settled,’’ Davidson said. ‘‘Some of that is experience. Some of that is [because of his] little time out from a long, grinding season.</p><p>‘‘I’m really excited about the way [he] has trended. When you look at where he was a couple of years ago to where he was a year [ago to now] . . . the arrow is definitely pointed up.’’</p><h3>Notes</h3><p>The Hawks will wear No. 19 patches on the backs of their helmets for the rest of the season to honor late player and broadcaster <b>Troy Murray</b>.</p><p>• <b>Jeff Blashill</b> coached his 600th career NHL game Sunday, becoming the sixth<br>U.S.-born coach to do so.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: 2 men shot, 1 fatally, in Back of the Yards
entries.summary: <p>Two men were shot, one fatally, in the Back of the Yards on the South Side early Monday. </p><p>Two men, 19 and 20, were sitting in a parked vehicle in the 5000 block of South Justine Street around 12:45 a.m. when a dark-colored sedan approached and two gunmen opened fire, Chicago police said. </p><p>The younger man was shot multiple times in the body and pronounced dead at the scene, while the older man was shot in the back and was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center where he was in critical condition, police said. </p><p>Area 1 detectives were investigating. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Rev. Jesse Jackson angered Jewish community, then reached out for common ground
entries.summary: <p>The Rev. Jesse Jackson's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/obituaries/2026/02/17/rev-jesse-jackson-civil-rights-leader-rainbow-push-obituary" >passing at 84</a> came after the release of Henry Louis Gates Jr.’s new PBS series, <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/show/black-and-jewish-america-an-interwoven-history/?gad_source=1&gad_campaignid=23480324820&gbraid=0AAAAAooDyp19OuVDETLWmQaWAOEUpp0Fb&gclid=CjwKCAiAzZ_NBhAEEiwAMtqKyzWyUTWstTexWO3_d7CZPtC1YCcJxiP2er3-zUChLY41TcduP5qLERoCAxQQAvD_BwE" target="_blank" >“Black and Jewish America: An Interwoven History.</a>" It brings to the fore the Black-Jewish allyship, which has frayed, along with the need to stitch it back together.</p><p>An iconic civil rights activist and political influencer, Jackson’s obituary contains a reference to his <a class="Link" href="https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/politics/1984/08/17/reporter-talks-to-black-press-on-hymie-remark/44055d97-fc62-440d-b081-c41e55d64bd3/" target="_blank" >“Hymietown” comments </a>made to Milton Coleman, a Washington Post reporter in 1984. “All Hymie [read Jews] wants to talk about is Israel; every time I go to Hymie Town [read New York City], that’s all they want to talk about.”</p><p>This episode became a model for managing an explosive situation with important lessons for today.</p><p>My late husband, <a class="Link" href="https://tanenbaum.org/about-us/who-we-are/history/" target="_blank" >Rabbi Marc Tanenbaum</a>, was director of interreligious relations at the American Jewish Committee at the time of Jackson’s comments. Along with much of the Jewish community, he was outraged.</p><p>But rather than attacking Jackson as an antisemite, or Jackson attacking him as a Zionist, the two clergymen created an opportunity for learning, healing and reconciliation. Three years later, in 1987, at great personal risk, Jackson came to New York to meet Rabbi Marc on stage at Queens College's Colden Auditorium for the Black-Jewish People to People Project.</p><p>Outside the auditorium was a vociferous group of demonstrators, members of the extremist Jewish Defense League, who were brawling with the police. But both men appeared on that stage in the face of "threats, slanders and intimidations."</p><p>Both were open about the issues that divided them: Israel’s sale of arms to South Africa, and Jackson’s refusal to disavow Louis Farrakhan’s antisemitic pronouncements of Judaism as a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2019/5/8/18619012/nation-of-islam-leader-louis-farrakhan-shutdown-on-facebook-not-surprising" >“gutter religion.”</a></p><p>And yet, the evening ended with an affirmation of the important allyship between Black and Jewish people, with Jackson declaring: "The enemy is not within this room. … When we understand that the enemy is outside, then we understand, too, that our allies are inside. … Let there be no question that the alliance is real, the goals are shared, and our direction will be forward."</p><p>To my mind, the most important lesson to come out of the Jackson-Tanenbaum encounter is this: If Blacks and Jews are to restore an urgently needed allyship, both must desist from subjecting each other to litmus tests.</p><p><i>Georgette Bennett, founder and president, Tanenbaum Center for Interreligious Understanding, and founder and chair, Multifaith Alliance.</i></p><h3>Driving test as senior not as stressful as my teen experience </h3><p>Illinois Secretary of State Alexi Giannoulias ended my 64-year drought of road tests to renew my driver’s license. With my license expiring in two weeks on my 81st birthday, I needed to take <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/springfield/2025/05/23/illinois-senior-road-tests-bill" >the road test</a>.</p><p>The nice lady next to me in my Subaru Crosstrek was very clear: "Follow my instructions precisely." No wiggle room there. I jettisoned my normal, somewhat aggressive driving style to little more than a crawl and passed in a quick 10 minutes.</p><p>During the test, my mental "wayback machine" went to the spring of 1962. Pop, who taught me how to drive as there was only "Rules of the Road" instruction at Kelly High School, took me to the West Side Department of Motor Vehicles facility off the Congress Expressway for the driving test.</p><p>With only a couple hundred miles on my driving logbook, I was quite nervous and intimidated when the burly driving tester replaced Pop in the car now under my control.</p><p>Passing was critical, as my senior prom was just weeks away, and I was scheduled to drive Joanie Downtown to the Sheraton Chicago Hotel for my first big boy-girl social event. Passing the dreaded driving test to get my license was a life crisis.</p><p>Overwhelmed, I flunked. In great distress, I sat stunned as we left the DMV. But less than a block away, Pop stopped the car and shouted, "We’re going back. …You can do this." Buoyed with Pop’s confidence, I took the second test determined to pass. And I did.</p><p>Good thing I didn't have a similar adventure with the road test this time. After passing last week, I asked my tester if many people flunk. "Oh yes," she said, "and they have to come back on another day for a do-over." Glad I didn’t have to face the anxiety of waiting another day 64 years ago.</p><p>All in all, 1962 turned out to be a pretty good year. Got my license due to the teaching and inspiration of Pop and got to drive Joanie Downtown to the senior prom. Thanks, Pop.</p><p><i>Walt Zlotow, Glen Ellyn</i></p><h3>Warmonger</h3><p>President Donald Trump's decision to change the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2025/04/23/pete-hegseth-defense-secretary-pentagon-trump-fox-signal-woke-s-e-cupp" >Department of Defense</a> to the <a class="Link" href="https://www.history.com/articles/department-of-war-defense-name-changes" target="_blank" >Department of War</a> was not a play on words. It was an indication of his goals.</p><p>This president will not only invade Venezuela and instigate a regime change in Iran, but he will make moves on Cuba, the Panama Canal Zone and Greenland. This is not a president of "peace" but a modern "warlord" like his pal Vladimir Putin.</p><p>If you are an investor, invest in the companies that make up our military industrial complex.</p><p><i>Warren Rodgers Jr., Orland Park</i> </p><h3>Barron can help dad achieve military goals </h3><p>Now that Barron Trump is approaching his 20th birthday, perhaps it’s time to mimic his old man’s vaunted patriotism and enlist in the military, instead of waiting for a reinstitution of the draft. No doubt he may catch a draft or develop a <a class="Link" href="https://www.presidency.ucsb.edu/documents/haley-campaign-press-release-donald-trump-and-the-mystery-the-spontaneous-bone-spurs" target="_blank" >bone spur</a> if that were to occur. However, with his collegiate background, perhaps he could gain a commission in today’s Army, thus guaranteeing our next war has a definitive plan for execution, not just to last <a class="Link" href="https://thehill.com/homenews/administration/5763127-trump-us-military-iran-operations/" target="_blank" >four to five weeks or “to go far longer than that.”</a></p><p><i>Lawrence Pietras, Glen Ellyn</i></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: More state funding for universities is an investment in Illinois' future
entries.summary: <p>A college degree is still the <a class="Link" href="https://ws.engr.illinois.edu/sitemanager/getfile.asp?id=8600" target="_blank" >best pathway</a> to future economic security and better opportunities. However, for many students, affordability continues to be the most significant barrier to enrolling in a four-year university, especially for Latinos, of which only 1 in 5 adults hold a bachelor's degree, the lowest rate of any group in Illinois, according to a <a class="Link" href="https://latinopolicyforum.org/wp-content/uploads/pub-202309-il-latino-college-landscape-study.pdf" target="_blank" >study</a> by the Latino Policy Forum.</p><p>This is a crisis that demands action, which is why we support efforts to pass the <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?DocNum=13&GAID=18&DocTypeID=SB&LegId=157103&SessionID=114&GA=104" target="_blank" >Adequate and Equitable Public University Funding Act</a>.</p><p>This bill would establish Illinois' first postsecondary funding formula and is built on three principles: adequacy, which accounts for each university's unique mission and costs; equity, which prioritizes support for underrepresented students and underfunded institutions; and stability, which protects universities from cuts while gradually achieving adequate funding over 10 to 15 years.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d30000" name="module-d30000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>There are many reasons why affording college is so challenging in Illinois, but among the biggest is disinvestment. In the early 2000s, Illinois began cutting investments in higher education, which led to enrollment declines and forced state universities to raise tuition and limit student support. This has made the cost of a higher education inaccessible for many students, especially Black, Latino, rural and low-income students, and much harder for the students remaining to persist and graduate.</p><p>Without adequate funding to offset costs, students are left with fewer resources, higher tuition costs and reduced support services. Campuses are asked to do more with less, innovation slows and access becomes more limited, especially for first-generation students and working-class families.</p><p>Today, Illinois tuition and fees are significantly higher than the national average and require 19% of the median household income, research by multiple organizations shows. For families with disproportionately low incomes, college costs pose a prohibitive barrier to access.</p><h3>Federal pressure, cuts also squeeze schools</h3><h3></h3><p>All of this is unfolding against the backdrop of a volatile federal government whose shifting approach to student financial aid and grant funding places even greater stress on state institutions that can least afford it. Recent federal reductions in research funding; diversity, equity and inclusion programs; and support for Hispanic-serving institutions at our public universities — combined with attacks on undocumented and international students — are disrupting college enrollment across the country.</p><p>These federal cuts deepen Illinois’ higher education funding crisis and threaten our future workforce. At a time when campuses in Illinois need stability, we must continue to fund the programs and pathways that support student success, help students graduate and sustain our workforce pipeline.</p><p>The Adequate and Equitable Public University Funding Act is a balanced solution that would provide sustainable funding for public universities, enabling the state to strengthen institutions and support students on their path to a degree. And although the bill would require an additional $135 million annually for 10 to 15 years, it is entirely feasible when you put the price tag in perspective. The cost of providing our students equitable access to a four-year program is <i>less than a quarter of 1%</i> of our entire state budget, and it becomes even more feasible if Gov. Pritzker releases the full 3% annual funding increase already allocated for public four-year institutions. Our lawmakers must prioritize this.</p><p>The question is not whether we can afford to make this investment. It is whether we can afford the long-term consequences of failing to do so.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://drive.google.com/file/d/1PSA788bEf_YLkzxsx-kzVpV6FgmZOFIF/view" target="_blank" >Updated projections</a> show the legislation could generate 122,200 more bachelor’s degree holders over 15 years, increase annual state tax revenue by $691.5 million, produce $156 million in yearly state savings in public assistance and incarceration costs, and drive $5.3 billion in additional local economic activity each year. That’s the economic impact of ensuring students, especially those with the greatest need, can enroll in and complete college. Illinois needs that growth and opportunity to remain competitive and strong.</p><p>Illinois must act now before federal uncertainty pushes another generation away from higher education. Without stronger state investment, families may look out of state — or decide college isn’t possible at all. If the <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?DocNum=13&GAID=18&DocTypeID=SB&LegId=157103&SessionID=114&GA=104" target="_blank" >Adequate and Equitable Public University Funding Act</a> fails to pass, our public universities could face closures. The future of our state depends on ensuring that every student who aspires to earn a degree has a real opportunity to do so.</p><p>Yes, Illinois faces budget pressures. But adequately funding higher education is not an expense; it is a way to shield students from federal instability and invest in the state’s economic future.</p><p>Without it, all Illinois students will face growing uncertainty. The new formula would offer a path toward financial stability and a prosperous Illinois by strengthening all public four-year institutions and, most importantly, the students they serve.</p><p><i>State Rep. Norma Hernandez serves the 77th District and is a member of the House Higher Education Committee. Jennifer Juárez, Ph.D., is director of higher education policy at the Latino Policy Forum.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b70000" name="module-b70000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Jennifer Juárez

entries.title: Dear Abby: Aging mother refuses to acknowledge risk of falling
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I moved across the country nine years ago to be closer to my parents as they age. They are now in their early to mid-80s. My mother has recently had a few falls. One was bad enough that she had to be taken by ambulance to the ER because she was not able to stand. (I suspect some of her falling is due to having a drink or two at a casino, which affects her balance.)</p><p>I have been saying for almost a year that my mother needs a walker. Because it has fallen on deaf ears, I took it upon myself to buy her a walker and shower chair. She and Dad are still convinced she doesn't need a walker. Abby, when we got to the hospital, she needed assistance to use the bathroom because she couldn't stand or walk on her own after the fall.</p><p>Today I learned she and Dad went to a casino without the walker. I am beside myself! They are either in denial or just don't want to listen to me, but I am at my wits' end because I'm sure this will happen again. I'm afraid her next fall may result in broken bones or worse. Any advice? — <i>FEARFUL DAUGHTER IN LAS VEGAS</i></p><p><b>DEAR DAUGHTER:</b> You may be trying to reason with the wrong parent. Assuming they are both in their right minds, tell your father that with the history of falls your mother has, the result of the next one could be serious. Neither of them is a spring chicken. If either one, particularly your mother, were to break a hip and be bedridden, it could lead to pneumonia and death. If they make light of it, get their doctor to weigh in.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My husband and I have been friends with the "Joneses" for many years. We traditionally exchange gift baskets. I enjoy picking out treats and items I think they would enjoy when assembling their basket. I also give them home-baked items.</p><p>Last year, their basket for us contained a freebie calendar from a charity (we also received one from the charity; they gifted us one the previous year as well, and I had told them then that we already had one). There was also an amenity bar of soap from a hotel, a summer sausage, a small wheel of processed cheese and a 4-inch-by-5-inch print the wife bought at an art fair I had attended with her years before. It seemed like she just went around her house gathering things she didn't want for the basket.</p><p>This year, I'm thinking about telling them we would rather not exchange gifts as it seems they can't be bothered. Do you think I'm being grinchy? — <i>THOUGHT THAT COUNTS?</i></p><p><b>DEAR THOUGHT:</b> Your friends may be experiencing financial difficulty. I do not think it would be kind or polite to tell them "it seems they can't be bothered." A better way to approach it might be to tell the wife that you and your husband are "cutting back on gift-giving this year," which is why you will be gifting friends a home-baked pie, cake, batch of cookies, etc., and you hope they will consider doing the same. (By the way, they already ARE cutting back, which is why you wrote to me!)</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>For everything you need to know about wedding planning, order "How to Have a Lovely Wedding." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Wedding Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: A new book unearths the influence of Margaret C. Anderson, early champion of literary greats
entries.summary: <p>Writer Adam Morgan knew nothing about Margaret C. Anderson when he “bumped into her ghost by accident” along a shadowy corridor on the ninth floor of downtown’s Fine Arts Building in 2008.</p><p>There — in the building where Frank Lloyd Wright and William Wallace Denslow, the original illustrator for “The Wonderful Wizard of Oz” books, had studios — was a small plaque dedicated to a woman that history has largely forgotten.</p><p>But, as Morgan would discover during research for his new book, Anderson was a woman whose iron will, rebellious streak and ability to spot genius where others saw only nauseating filth, helped change the way Chicago and the world sees literature today.</p><p>“She knew that great art required taking big risks, which is easily forgotten today in the era of corporate … publishing. She did not care what anyone thought about her writing or the things she was publishing,” said Morgan, author of the recently released “A Danger to the Minds of Young Girls: Margaret C. Anderson, Book Bans, and The Fight to Modernize Literature” (Simon & Schuster; $29).</p><p>Morgan spent 10 years in Chicago, where he founded the “Chicago Review of Books” and became fascinated by Anderson, founder of the literary and arts magazine, “The Little Review,” first published in 1914.</p><p>“She was a queer woman who was often dismissed, particularly by male scholars and critics, as being this flighty society woman because she was also staggeringly beautiful,” said Morgan, who now lives in North Carolina.</p><p>Anderson’s genius was in finding the promise in “fringe, bohemian weirdos that nobody else wanted to publish at the time,” Morgan said.</p><p>In Anderson’s magazine, American readers were introduced — often at considerable risk to herself — to the likes of Ezra Pound, T. S. Eliot and the great Irish modernist James Joyce. Anderson also wrote about her admiration for feminist and anarchist Emma Goldman, described at the time as “the most dangerous woman in America.” Goldman would later be charged and convicted under the Espionage Act of 1917, resulting in a two-year prison sentence.</p><p>Morgan reconstructs the story of how Anderson escaped to Chicago after a suffocating upbringing in a suburb of Indianapolis. Chicago was a young city unfettered by an old-world mindset but with a luminous cultural landscape of the recently built Art Institute of Chicago, Orchestra Hall, Field Museum and the Newberry Library.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-cc0000" name="image-cc0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/438c068/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+1390/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F31%2F20%2F49aef73e4474abc19d2dd247135a%2Fadam-morgan-pc-beowulf-sheehan.jpg
