width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f8c7452/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+1390/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F31%2F20%2F49aef73e4474abc19d2dd247135a%2Fadam-morgan-pc-beowulf-sheehan.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Adam Morgan pc Beowulf Sheehan.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f8c7452/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+1390/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F31%2F20%2F49aef73e4474abc19d2dd247135a%2Fadam-morgan-pc-beowulf-sheehan.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5064dc0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+1390/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F31%2F20%2F49aef73e4474abc19d2dd247135a%2Fadam-morgan-pc-beowulf-sheehan.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f8c7452/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+1390/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F31%2F20%2F49aef73e4474abc19d2dd247135a%2Fadam-morgan-pc-beowulf-sheehan.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“[Anderson] was a queer woman who was often dismissed, particularly by male scholars and critics, as being this flighty society woman because she was also staggeringly beautiful,” said Adam Morgan, author of “A Danger to the Minds of Young Girls: Margaret C. Anderson, Book Bans, and The Fight to Modernize Literature,” published in Dec. 2025. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Beowulf Sheehan</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Constantly in need of cash to keep her struggling magazine afloat, Anderson wasn’t averse to dangling her charms, even though she had no interest in the men who succumbed to them, Morgan writes.</p><p>In a time when homosexuality was illegal across the United States, Anderson never let the very real threat of prison douse her romantic impulses, having affairs with the American writer Solita Solano, opera singer and actress Georgette Leblanc and Dorothy Caruso, the widow of Italian opera star Enrico Caruso, among others.</p><p>Morgan portrays Anderson as a woman with an unwavering loyalty to the writers she championed, in particular Joyce. When she first read “Ulysses,” Joyce’s modernist masterpiece, she was determined to publish it in serial form, uncensored.</p><p>“This is the most beautiful thing we’ll ever have,” Anderson told Jane Heap, her one-time lover and collaborator at “The Little Review.” “We’ll print it if it’s the last effort of our lives.”</p><p>Anderson was true to her word, but America was not ready for Joyce’s prose, which Morgan quotes liberally: “Wildly I lay on her, kissed her: eyes, her lips, her stretched neck beating, woman’s breasts full in her blouse of nun’s veiling, fat nipples upright.”</p><p>Anderson was charged with violating the Comstock Act, a set of federal anti-obscenity laws. In court, she was accused of publishing “scenes of lewdness and of obscenity” in her “disgusting magazine.”</p><p>She was found guilty and fined $50.</p><p>The verdict left Anderson disheartened. Worse was to come, when “Ulysses,” having been mocked stateside, was published to great acclaim in Paris. Anderson’s hope was that art and literature — “Ulysses,” specifically — would change the way America saw the world and itself. For all her effort, she felt she’d failed.</p><p>“She became very disillusioned with that, including the fact that she felt like those grenades blew everything up but didn’t replace what was there with anything else,” Morgan said.</p><p>Anderson spent much of the rest of her life living in France, still chasing the beautiful life but having given up publishing.</p><p>“In her youth, she was the tip of this experimental, countercultural spear,” Morgan said. “As an old woman, looking at the counterculture of the 1960s, say, she finds the young people of that era obnoxious and weird, and thinks their art is terrible.”</p><p>That shouldn’t detract from her legacy, Morgan said. “Without her, I don’t think we would have literary modernism as we know it today. I don’t think we would have ‘Ulysses’ as we know it today. … Or Chicago,” Morgan said, pointing out that everyone else had rejected the novel until Anderson took a chance with the work.</p><p>Anderson died in 1973, at 86, in a hospital in southeast France.</p>
entries.author: Stefano Esposito

entries.title: Want to be vibrantly alive in your 90s? Here’s how my dad does it
entries.summary: <p>My father told me recently that he is not long for this world.</p><p>He asked me to track down a particular book on the subject of what happens to the mind and body as the end approaches, which, thankfully, appears to be out of print.</p><p>Still, in mid-February I dutifully boarded a flight from Chicago headed for London, the first leg of a journey that would end in Florence, Italy, where my father lives, to celebrate his 94th birthday.</p><p>Here’s the thing about Papa: Like many Italians, he has a flair for the melodramatic.</p><p>He’s been warning of his imminent passing for at least the past 10 years. He remains, though, among the most alive human beings I have ever met.</p><p>Papa talks to everyone: the butcher, the barista at his favorite coffee bar, a professor of Renaissance literature, a complete stranger one quarter his age. These aren’t people merely humoring an old man; he flashes his hazel eyes — speaking of his youth on Capri, the craggy island jutting from the turquoise waters of the Tyrrhenian Sea, or perhaps the 15th-century mill in Southwest England he renovated in the 1980s — and his listeners are entranced. Much the same way when years before, with microphone in hand, he charmed American, Australian and South African passengers as their tour director on coaches crisscrossing Europe.</p><div 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1dea443/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3024x1697+0+663/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3b%2F8f%2F9c3c55624e278c448db570a3d25a%2Fimg-5015.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Papa stopping off to pick up a few groceries in Florence. All of the shopkeepers know him by name and his very particular requests for pumpkin, flax and chia seeds.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Stefano Esposito/Sun-Times </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>When I arrived in Porta Romana, his neighborhood in Florence, a few days before his birthday, I found Papa’s face a little more creased, and he walked with a cane now (il bastone, in Italian). But he was still spritzed with expensive Italian cologne, still wearing cashmere sweaters and still driving — less terrifying these days because he no longer tears the wrong way down one-way streets when he’s in a hurry.</p><p>And he still gives orders he expects me to obey.</p><p>“I want you to give a speech for my birthday — in Italian,” he said.</p><p>“Yes, Papa,” I replied, even though I am not fluent.</p><p>Sadly, I cannot report my father survives only on pasta and wine (although he drinks at least a glass daily). He begins each morning with a sludgy concoction of ground-up pumpkin, flax, chia seeds and honey, all swirled into yogurt. He raves, too, about the medicinal powers of “curcuma” (turmeric).</p><p>On the morning of his birthday, Feb. 19, Papa hobbled down the hill, through a 14th-century stone gate (a remnant of the great defensive wall that at one time surrounded Florence) and stepped into his favorite coffee bar. Eliza, the barista, began a round of the Italian version of Happy Birthday, “Tanti auguri a te …”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c37b8f0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3648x2047+0+1712/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F32%2F65%2F884750034de2ba4f76de2d5224df%2Fimg-1339.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Papa and I visiting a winery a short drive from Florence. Papa still drives and he still drinks plenty of wine. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Stefano Esposito</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Papa loved the attention. His 88-year-old friend Massimo arrived. So did Francesco, who is 80.</p><p>A little later, we headed to a local osteria. It was packed. So we shared a table with strangers, a man and woman in their early 30s. Soon, the man had his arm around Papa. I told the restaurant owner it was my father’s birthday. A slice of cake with a candle on top arrived. Another round of “Tanti auguri.” Like a conductor, Papa directed the hundred or so people in the restaurant.</p><p>Later that night, there was a small gathering of friends at the condominium my father shares with my stepmother, Jane. One friend, Fabio, dressed up as a university professor and, with a pie chart, tried to explain the secret to my father’s abundant good health. The largest slices of pie: curiosity, turmeric and sex.</p><p>I talked about Papa’s awful driving, his total lack of inhibition. A few years ago, along the Chicago River, he heard a James Brown song he likes and began to dance, all by himself as dozens of strangers strolled by.</p><p>“E mamma mia, lui piaceva proprio tanto parlare!” I said, explaining how my father adores talking.</p><p>I shared the story of how as a little kid living in London, I desperately needed to get home to pee. My father heard me but then spent the next half hour talking to a man in an antiques shop. He had to carry me home, pants wet, on his shoulders.</p><p>Papa took it all in — eyebrows raised and arms flung out in mock disbelief — but he knew it was all true.</p><p>On another evening, a couple of nights before I headed back to Chicago, we were having dinner with friends. Papa heard a clattering Latin rhythm. He rose to his feet, cell phone in one hand and cane in the other, and he started to dance, hips swiveling. He shifted to a stuttering, creaky step — mocking the expectations of old age.</p><p>Papa told me on this trip he doesn’t believe in life after death. I don’t know if he’s right. But I know Papa is leaving nothing to chance — living his life completely until his very last breath.</p>
entries.author: Stefano Esposito

entries.title: Chicagoan rebuilds life after deportation
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p><b>Below</b>: One longtime Little Village resident tries to rebuild, after being deported, in a hometown that he hasn't seen in decades.</p><p><b>Plus</b>: A Colorado program for people with mental illness draws interest in Illinois for both its successes and low price tag, Richard Roeper is back with Oscars predictions, and more.</p><p><b>Keeping score</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjuiidy-tlkjhhnv-j/" target="_blank" >Bulls fall to Kings</a>, 126-110; <a class="Link" Torreon, Coahuila, Mexico. Wedneday, Feb. 25, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Duilio Rodriguez</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjuiidy-tlkjhhnv-h/" target="_blank" >Labeled a gang member and deported, longtime Chicagoan tries to restart in Mexican hometown</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjuiidy-tlkjhhnv-k/" target="_blank" >Adriana Cardona-Maguigad | WBEZ</a></h5><p><b>End of the line</b>: Francisco Gonzalez-Jasso, a longtime Little Village resident, didn’t know exactly how to make his way back to his childhood home in Torreón, a city in Mexico’s northern state Coahuila. He hadn’t been there in 33 years. "I kept asking people on the bus, 'Is Torreón next?'" Gonzalez-Jasso says in Spanish.</p><p><b>What happened next</b>: Gonzalez-Jasso, 55, is trying to rebuild his life in Mexico after he was arrested by U.S. Border Patrol agents in October in Little Village, in front of friends and neighbors. He was deported a few days later. WBEZ has been keeping up with Gonzalez-Jasso after visiting him in Mexico in December, not long after he arrived.</p><p><b>Rejects accusation</b>: Gonzalez-Jasso is among hundreds of people swept up during Operation Midway Blitz, the Trump administration's deportation campaign aimed at clearing "violent criminals" off Chicago-area streets. He's also among those who say they were wrongly accused of being criminals or having ties to gangs. "It’s humiliating for me," Gonzalez-Jasso says. "I’m not a criminal. I never harmed anyone."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/immigration/2026/03/08/labeled-gang-member-deported-longtime-chicagoan-restart-mexican-hometown-ice-midway-blitz" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h3>EDUCATION 📚</h3><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2f0000" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjuiidy-tlkjhhnv-o/" target="_blank" >Aspira will soon have no teachers or money while CPS struggles to close it</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjuiidy-tlkjhhnv-b/" target="_blank" >Sarah Karp | WBEZ</a></h5><p><b>Classroom quagmire</b>: Chicago Public Schools is trying to transfer some 545 students out of a charter school network that has refused to officially close, even though it will run out of money next month. Advocates and officials are now debating the adequacy of charter schools' funding and raising questions about their accountability.</p><p><b>Education in limbo</b>: CPS officials made it clear they think students should abandon Aspira. They sent letters to families saying students will be offered seats at a nearby school for the remainder of the year. Still, officials noted that Aspira’s charter is not being revoked "at this time."</p><p><b>'Very fine line'</b>: CPS interim CEO and Superintendent Macquline King said the school district was in a tricky position. "[Aspira has] to elect to self-close or CPS will be in violation of their contract," she said. "We are walking a very fine line to respond in a way that is humane and acceptable ... while honoring the contract.”</p><p><b>Network pushback</b>: "Stability matters right now," officials from the Illinois Network of Charter Schools said in a statement. “Keeping both [Aspira] campuses open through the end of the academic year ... would avoid the obvious negative consequences of moving high schoolers to an unfamiliar school just weeks ahead of graduation."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/05/aspira-will-soon-have-no-teachers-or-money-cps-is-still-struggling-to-close-it" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h3>THE WATCHDOGS 🔍</h3><div data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Lottie Elliott, who says she has benefited from the Bridges of Colorado program, stands near the spot where she spent her first night homeless in Monument Valley Park in Colorado Springs, Monday, Feb. 23, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Kevin Mohatt/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjuiidy-tlkjhhnv-m/" target="_blank" >Illinois lawmakers look to Colorado program that, for $6, a day helps mentally ill people</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjuiidy-tlkjhhnv-c/" target="_blank" >Stephanie Zimmermann</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjuiidy-tlkjhhnv-q/" target="_blank" >Frank Main</a></h5><p><b>A way out</b>: Decades of undiagnosed mental illness beginning when Lottie Elliott was 15 led her to homelessness, her children being taken away and repeated arrests. In 2022, Elliott was offered a way to stop that damaging cycle through Bridges of Colorado, which places mental health liaisons in courtrooms across the state to help defendants whose persistent mental illness has contributed to repeated arrests.</p><p><b>Results</b>: Elliott says people from the Bridges program worked with her in jail until she was found to be competent to face criminal charges. The liaisons helped get her into a residential sober-living program with intensive therapy under the monitoring of a judge, who granted her probation. She’s now in her own apartment, has reunited with her son, is taking regular mental health medication and is helping others as a peer counselor.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: An Illinois task force of legislators, law enforcement, judiciary officials and mental health professionals is looking at Bridges of Colorado as a potential model for Illinois. They've been working to figure out how to improve the handling of criminal defendants with severe, persistent mental illness.</p><p><b>Chance to thrive</b>: "My life is incredibly humbling," Elliott says. "I want to share with people who were like me, who believed there was no hope and it was not possible. It is. We can recover."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/06/mentally-ill-downtown-killings-chicago-police-department-mental-health-care-bridges-colorado" Pictures shows director-writer-producer Ryan Coogler, left, and cinematographer Autumn Durald Arkapaw on the set of “Sinners.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Eli Adé/AP Photos</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjuiidy-tlkjhhnv-jj/" target="_blank" >Richard Roeper’s 2026 Oscars predictions are here</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjuiidy-tlkjhhnv-jt/" target="_blank" >Richard Roeper</a></h5><p>The 98th annual Academy Awards are a week away, so let’s look at some of the pressing questions about this year’s ceremony — and my picks in the four acting categories plus best picture.</p><p>I expect to see "One Battle After Another" and "Sinners" split the evening with five total wins apiece, "Frankenstein" taking home as many as four Oscars in four craft categories, "KPop Demon Hunters" winning for best animated feature and original song, and nine other films/performances winning one Academy Award.</p><p>That so many of the major categories are still up for grabs this late should make for one of the most entertaining and unpredictable Oscars in recent years.</p><p>Here are my predictions for who will win — and opinions on who should win — in five major categories. (After clicking below, scroll to the bottom of the story for a complete, downloadable 2026 Oscars ballot, with Roeper’s picks in bold.)</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/movies-tv/2026/03/08/richard-roepers-2026-oscars-predictions-are-here" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h3>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h3><p><b>Daily Q: What's the most memorable name for a pet you've encountered? entries.title: At least 7 killed in weekend homicides citywide
entries.summary: <p>At least seven people were killed throughout Chicago in weekend violence from 5 p.m. Friday to 5 a.m. Monday. Two of the attacks were double homicides.</p><p>The murders, which occurred in the Back of the Yards, Austin, North Lawndale, River North and Washington Heights, left victims from age 19 to age 71 dead, according to Chicago police.<br></p><ul class="ul1" id="rte-50401c60-1bb7-11f1-be59-9987a0089542"><li>A 35-year-old man was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/09/man-trying-to-intervene-in-fight-fatally-pushed-down-flight-of-stairs-in-river-north" target="_blank" >pushed down a flight of stairs and killed</a> while trying to intervene in a fight early Monday in River North. A man and a group of people were fighting around 1:20 a.m. when the man pushed the unidentified victim down the stairs in the 100 block of West Hubbard Street, police said. The man was taken to Northwestern Memorial Hospital where he died. The 32-year-old man who allegedly pushed the victim was arrested at the scene and charges were pending.<br></li><li>Less than an hour earlier, two men were fatally shot in the Back of the Yards on the South Side. The two men, both 19, were sitting in a parked vehicle in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/09/2-men-shot-1-fatally-in-back-of-the-yards" target="_blank" >5000 block of South Justine Street</a> around 12:45 a.m. when a dark-colored sedan approached and two gunmen opened fire, police said. The younger man was shot multiple times in the body and pronounced dead at the scene, while the older man was shot in the back and was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center where he later died, according to police.<br></li><li>On Sunday night, a man was found fatally shot in a vehicle in Austin on the West Side. Around 7:50 p.m., the 31-year-old man was found shot inside the vehicle in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/08/chicago-west-side-austin-man-in-vehicle-found-fatally-shot" target="_blank" >4700 block of West Arthington Street</a>, police said. He was taken to Mount Sinai Hospital with a gunshot wound to his chest and was pronounced dead, police said.<br></li><li>Several hours earlier, a 25-year-old man was fatally shot in a North Lawndale home, authorities said. The man was arguing with another person at the home in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/08/man-fatally-shot-north-lawndale-home" target="_blank" >2100 block of South Trumbull Avenue</a> when the person pulled out a gun and shot the man three times in the chest about 8 a.m., according to police. He was taken to Mount Sinai Hospital where he was pronounced dead.<br></li><li>Two women were fatally shot in the far South Side Washington Heights neighborhood Saturday night. Officers found the women — 68-year-old Phyllis Goolsby and 71-year-old Tuanna Thomas — fatally shot in the chest in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/08/two-women-fatally-shot-washington-heights" target="_blank" >10400 block of South Peoria Street</a> before 11 p.m., according to the Cook County medical examiner’s office and police. They were both pronounced dead at the scene.</li></ul>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: Man trying to intervene in fight fatally pushed down flight of stairs in River North
entries.summary: <p>Charges were pending for someone who allegedly pushed a 35-year-old man down a flight of stairs early Monday during a fight at a River North business.</p><p>The 35-year-old tried to intervene in the fight between a man and group of people that occurred around 1:20 a.m. at 110 W. Hubbard St.when another man pushed him pushed down the stairs, Chicago police said. A multi-story building is located at 110 W. Hubbard lists at least one nightclub and a restaurant.</p><p>The unidentified man was taken to Northwestern Memorial Hospital where he was pronounced dead, police said. </p><p>The 32-year-old man who allegedly pushed the victim was arrested at the scene and charges were pending. </p><p>Area 3 detectives are investigating.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Bears to sign Seahawks standout safety Coby Bryant
entries.summary: <p>Coby Bryant, the standout safety who helped guide the Seahawks to the Super Bowl title last season, is the newest <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bear</a>.</p><p>The team will sign him when the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/08/bears-transaction-tracker-2026-offseason-free-agency-coming-going-ryan-poles-ben-johnson" target="_blank" >league year begins Wednesday</a>, a source confirmed. The three-year deal expected to be worth $40 million, per ESPN.</p><p>Pro Football Focus graded Bryant as the 27<sup>th</sup>-best safety out of 91 candidates last year and the 22<sup>nd</sup>-best out of 98 the year before. He’s best known as a stout run stopper but had four interceptions, seven pass deflections and one forced fumble last year as an integral part of the NFL’s best defense. He had four tackles in the Seahawks’ 29-13 Super Bowl victory against the Patriots.</p><p>Bryant, who turns 27 at the end of this month, was a fourth-round pick out of Cincinnati in 2022. He was drafted as a cornerback before being moved to safety. </p><p>The Bears entered the offseason with a dire need at safety, having their top four players — starters Kevin Byard and Jaquan Brisker and backups Jonathan Owens and Elijah Hicks — coming out contract. It’s unclear whether Bryant’s addition precludes the Bears from bringing back Byard, a captain last year. Both sides expressed optimism earlier this offseason that they’d get a deal done. It would be unusual for the Bears to devote major free-agent contracts to both the starting free safety and strong safety positions.</p><p>Byard said he wanted to return to Chicago but was clear he’d take family into consideration. His wife and kids spend half the year in Nashville, where he played as a member of the Titans. Former Titans head coach Mike Vrabel is now the coach in New England, another spot that could be appealing. Byard said he wants to go somewhere where he can win.</p><p>Bryant is named after former Lakers great Kobe Bryant and wears his No. 8 jersey.</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Man, 75, allegedly confessed to fatally shooting two women in Washington Heights: police report
entries.summary: <p>Charges were pending Monday for a suspect, 75, who allegedly confessed to police that he fatally shot his girlfriend and another woman he lived in the city's Far South Side Washington Park neighborhood.</p><p>A motive remained unknown for the late Saturday attack. </p><p>Officers said they responded to a 911 call from the alleged shooter about 11 p.m. and when they knocked on the door of the home in the <a class="Link" href="https://www.suntimeswire.com/crime/2026/03/08/two-women-fatally-shot-washington-heights" >10400 block of South Peoria Street </a> the 75-year-old suspect "spontaneously" told police that he shot the women and the gun was "upstairs," according to a Chicago police report. </p><p>The officers found 68-year-old Phyllis Goolsby unresponsive with gunshot wounds to the chest on the second floor, according to the report, which said Goolsby and the alleged shooter were in a romantic relationship with no prior domestic incidents. </p><p>Officers said they found another victim, a still-unidentified 71-year-old woman, unresponsive in the first floor bedroom with gunshot wounds to her back and torso, the report said.</p><p>Several shell casings lie around the bodies of the women, who lived with the alleged shooter and were dead on the scene. </p><p>The 75-year-old was taken into custody and a handgun was found and secured on the second floor of the home, the report said. </p><p>Police said his charges were pending Monday. <br></p>
entries.author: Elleiana Green

entries.title: Bears' first move in free agency is deal with DT Neville Gallimore: source
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> opened free agency Monday by agreeing to a deal with former Colts defensive tackle Neville Gallimore, a source said. He'll join the team on a two-year, $12 million contract when deals can become official Wednesday.</p><p>Gallimore, 29, is a solid pass rusher who will be playing his seventh season. The Bears will add him to a group that includes veteran <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2025/03/10/bears-continue-reinforcing-defensive-line-by-adding-2-time-pro-bowl-dt-grady-jarrett-source" >Grady Jarrett</a> and former second-round pick Gervon Dexter. Andrew Billings is a free agent who could return as well.</p><p>Gallimore played every game for the Colts last season, starting eight and playing 40.2% of their defensive snaps. He had 38 tackles, three pass breakups and a career-high 3 1/2 sacks. He also played on special teams.</p><p>Pro Football Focus ranked him 77th among defensive tackles and 31st at his position as a pass rusher.</p><p>He grew up in Canada before playing at Oklahoma and was a third-round pick by the Cowboys in 2020. After four seasons with the Cowboys, he played for the Rams in 2024 and the Colts last season. In 83 career games, he has 147 tackles, 7 1/2 sacks and six pass breakups.</p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Former Bears QB Mitch Trubisky to sign with Titans
entries.summary: <p>Former <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> quarterback Mitch Trubisky will have a new home in 2026: Nashville, Tenn.</p><p>He agreed to sign a two-year deal with the Titans, per NFL Network, on the first day of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/08/bears-transaction-tracker-2026-offseason-free-agency-coming-going-ryan-poles-ben-johnson" target="_blank" >legal tampering</a> in the NFL. He'll serve as a backup quarterback to Cam Ward, who was the No. 1 overall pick last year. He'll play for Robert Saleh, who was named head coach in January, and coordinator Brian Daboll.</p><p>Trubisky knows Daboll's system, having played for him in 2021, which was the quarterback's first tour with the Bills. Since leaving the Bears after the 2020 season, Trubisky played a year in Buffalko, then two in Pittsburgh and two more in Buffalo.</p><p>When Trubisky traveled to Halas Hall last year for a joint practice between the Bears and Bills, he reflected on playing quarterback in Chicago.</p><p>"There’s a lot of pressure that comes with that, but it’s a privilege," he said. “I miss that. It’s a lot of fun to be the franchise guy, especially in a city like this.”<br></p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Estereotipado como miembro de una pandilla y deportado, residente de Chicago intenta reiniciar su vida
entries.summary: <p>Francisco González Jasso, un residente de muchos años del vecindario de La Villita, no sabía exactamente cómo regresar a su casa de la infancia en Torreón, una ciudad en el estado de Coahuila, en el norte de México. No había estado allí en 33 años.</p><p>“Seguí preguntándole a la gente en el autobús, '¿Está Torreón cerca?'”, contó González Jasso, de 55 años.</p><p>En Torreón, tomó un taxi hacia su viejo vecindario. Pero los años habían borrado sus recuerdos de la casa. Caminó por la calle, revisando las direcciones en cada puerta. Cuando encontró la correcta, dice que la casa se veía diferente. Era realmente pequeña.</p><p>“Empecé a gritar el nombre de mi hermano: César, César”, agregó González Jasso. “Una vez que vi su cara, le dije al taxista que se fuera. Entonces, abracé a mi hermano.</p><p>“Estaba destrozado”.</p><p>La última vez que estuvieron juntos, César, ahora de 50 años, tenía 17.</p><p>González Jasso está tratando de reconstruir su vida en México después de que fue arrestado por agentes de la Patrulla Fronteriza el octubre pasado en La Villita, ante amigos y vecinos. Fue deportado unos días después. WBEZ ha estado siguiendo a González Jasso después de visitarlo en México en diciembre, poco después de su llegada.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/89872ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F91%2F700b43da45b4b6210841916112e5%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0172.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>González Jasso desayuna en casa de su hermano en febrero. Tiene una gran familia extendida en su ciudad natal, pero el ajuste de regreso a casa ha sido una lucha.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Duilio Rodríguez/Para el Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>El hombre alto y de complexión robusta fue una presencia bien conocida en su vecindario de Chicago durante más de tres décadas. Sin embargo, a pesar de no tener antecedentes criminales, el Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS) lo estereotipó como miembro de la pandilla Latin Kings, un estereotipo que lo ha seguido hasta México.</p><p>González Jasso forma parte de cientos de personas atrapadas durante el Operativo Midway Blitz, la agresiva campaña de deportación de la administración de Trump dirigida a limpiar las calles del área de Chicago de “criminales violentos”. También se encuentra entre los inmigrantes que dicen haber sido acusados erróneamente de ser criminales o de tener vínculos con pandillas.</p><p>“Es humillante para mí”, dice González Jasso. “No soy un criminal. Nunca le hice daño a nadie”.</p><p>‘Este era su hogar'</p><p>González Jasso era conocido en la Calle 26 en La Villita como Panchito. Su arresto y deportación el otoño pasado fueron un golpe para los vecinos que lo recuerdan como un rostro amistoso siempre dispuesto a ayudar.</p><p>“Lloré, honestamente, lloré cuando supe que Panchito había sido arrestado”, dijo Carlos Macías, el dueño de la Carnicería y Taquería Aguascalientes en la Calle 26. Los dos han sido amigos por más de 20 años.</p><p>Ana Guerrero, una mesera en la taquería que a menudo atendía a González Jasso, dijo, “Él era como tu café diario, siempre aquí en las mañanas antes de ir a trabajar. Siempre fue muy respetuoso. Este era su hogar”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2979c8" name="image-2979c8"></a> alt="González Jasso lleva una gorra de los Chicago White Sox en su ciudad natal en México," srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7f3cefd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F32%2F29%2Fa9417bc546409b94b56c67de1c31%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0451.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/09aa641/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F32%2F29%2Fa9417bc546409b94b56c67de1c31%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0451.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7f3cefd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F32%2F29%2Fa9417bc546409b94b56c67de1c31%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0451.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>González Jasso lleva un poco de Chicago con él a su ciudad natal en México, donde extraña la vida que construyó durante tres décadas en Chicago. También tiene ropa de los Sox y de los Blackhawks.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Duilio Rodríguez/Para el Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>La tarde de su arresto, dice González Jasso, él y sus amigos estaban en una fiesta de cumpleaños en Jacaranda, un bar en la Calle 26. Salió a comprar un paquete de cigarrillos. Fue entonces cuando los agentes de la Patrulla Fronteriza lo arrestaron.</p><p>“Lo más triste, y duele, es que me agarraron, me forzaron al suelo, me apuntaron con un arma, frente a la gente, a los niños”, dice González Jasso. “Tu mundo se derrumba. Tu vida se acabó. Atado, esposado, con dolor, decepcionado. ¿Qué hice?”.</p><p>Cuenta que los agentes de la Patrulla Fronteriza le pegaron varias veces mientras lo arrastraban hacia una camioneta. Los agentes no conocían su nombre, no le mostraron una orden y no tenían razón para detenerlo, dice. Un video de su arresto circuló en <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/100001704351957/videos/1482298379669220/" target="_blank" >las redes sociales</a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1ef6fc" name="image-1ef6fc"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/663a7b9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe6%2F8e%2F86a994fc43a58923922d8c2a527b%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0202.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Panchito_DuilioR_DSC0202.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/663a7b9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe6%2F8e%2F86a994fc43a58923922d8c2a527b%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0202.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0d453c0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe6%2F8e%2F86a994fc43a58923922d8c2a527b%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0202.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/663a7b9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe6%2F8e%2F86a994fc43a58923922d8c2a527b%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0202.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>González Jasso muestra el video de su arresto por agentes federales en Chicago. El video circuló en las redes sociales, difundiendo la noticia de su arresto y deportación antes de que llegara de regreso a casa.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Duilio Rodríguez/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>DHS emitió un comunicado esa tarde diciendo, “La Patrulla Fronteriza llevó a cabo una acción de cumplimiento dirigida que resultó en el arresto de Francisco González Jasso, un extranjero criminal de México y miembro de la organización criminal Latin Kings”.</p><p>La agencia no ha respondido a preguntas sobre qué evidencia tiene de esa afirmación.</p><p>El nombre de González Jasso no figura en los registros estatales o municipales como alguien con vínculos a pandillas o con antecedentes criminales. Tampoco fue incluido en el “Libro de Pandillas” de la Comisión de Crimen de Chicago de 2018, el más reciente publicado.</p><p>Vivió en Chicago sin estatus migratorio legal y fue deportado una vez antes, en la década de 1980. Pero dice que las acusaciones del gobierno que lo nombran como miembro de una pandilla no son ciertas. Trabajó en la construcción durante años y en lugares que requerían verificaciones de antecedentes, incluidos edificios escolares.</p><p>“Ya me hubieran atrapado después de 30 años”, dice. Los defensores dicen que González Jasso no es el único que fue etiquetado como criminal. DHS ha hecho otras acusaciones y ha presentado cargos contra algunos, solo para retirarlos más tarde por falta de pruebas.</p><p>Después de su arresto, dice González Jasso, lo llevaron al centro de procesamiento del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) en Broadview, donde los funcionarios “trataban a los animales mejor que a nosotros”.</p><p>No había suficiente comida o agua, dice, y le negaron su medicina. Estaba triste en una celda abarrotada. Temiendo que se quedara allí durante semanas, González Jasso se rindió y firmó sus papeles de deportación. Dice que fue enviado a centros de detención en cuatro estados antes de llegar a Matamoros, México.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6e834d" name="image-6e834d"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0c1c569/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fc4%2F23c2e6584224a3373642bc2dfc51%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0414.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d674570/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fc4%2F23c2e6584224a3373642bc2dfc51%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0414.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fcd1850/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fc4%2F23c2e6584224a3373642bc2dfc51%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0414.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="En la mesita de noche de González Jasso hay un retrato de sus padres, a quienes no ha visto desde que emigró a Estados Unidos hace más de 30 años; ya fallecieron." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fcd1850/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fc4%2F23c2e6584224a3373642bc2dfc51%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0414.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a1c4ec6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fc4%2F23c2e6584224a3373642bc2dfc51%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0414.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fcd1850/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fc4%2F23c2e6584224a3373642bc2dfc51%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0414.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>En la mesita de noche de González Jasso hay un retrato de sus padres. No pudo verlos después de migrar a los Estados Unidos hace más de 30 años; ya fallecieron.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Duilio Rodríguez/Para el Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>La noticia de su deportación llegó primero a México</p><p>González Jasso llegó a su ciudad natal en mal estado. No había dormido, ni se había bañado, ni cambiado de ropa en días. Tenía dolor por los golpes durante su arresto, dice.</p><p>Pasó dos días en la cama. Le tomó varias semanas volver a sentirse como él mismo, cuenta.</p><p>“Seguí pensando, ‘¿Qué voy a hacer?’”, agregó. “Fui deportado como el peor criminal. Mi reputación se ha ido”.</p><p>Y el hogar donde ahora vivía no era lo que recordaba. A lo largo de los años, había perdido a sus padres y a dos hermanos. Las cortinas descoloridas y los muebles desgastados eran un recordatorio constante de que habían pasado tres décadas, de que sus padres ya no estaban allí para mantener todo unido.</p><p>González Jasso dice que se sentía como un intruso allí a pesar de los esfuerzos de su hermano por hacerlo sentir bienvenido. César estaba viviendo allí con su esposa y tres hijos.</p><p>Su hermano y otros familiares habían visto los videos de su arresto incluso antes de que él llegara, lo que hizo que su integración fuera aún más difícil. Cuando su hermana se enteró de su arresto, estaba tan afectada que casi tuvo un colapso nervioso.</p><p>“No quería que nadie supiera que me habían echado así”, dice González Jasso.</p><p>Cuatro meses después de su deportación, González Jasso vive de una modesta pensión de su trabajo en construcción. Envío su ropa desde Chicago y regaló los muebles que dejó atrás. Está tratando de conseguir que alguien lleve sus dos autos.</p><p>Se mantiene ocupado remodelando la casa de sus padres, haciendo una renovación completa de la cocina y los dormitorios.</p><p>Pero le preocupa la violencia en México. Uno de sus hermanos desapareció hace años y nunca fue encontrado.</p><p>De vez en cuando, repite el video de su arresto. Es doloroso escuchar los sonidos: los silbidos, una mujer gritando “la migra” y verse rodeado de agentes de la Patrulla Fronteriza.</p><p>González Jasso quiere limpiar su nombre para que todos sepan que no es un miembro de una pandilla. Pero no sabe por dónde empezar.</p><p>Y extraña a sus amigos y vecinos de La Villita. Algunos de ellos viajaron recientemente a México para verlo durante unos días. No son sus familiares de sangre. Pero fueron su familia.</p><p>En Chicago, dice, con la voz entrecortada, “Hay muchas personas que me aman. Y los extraño”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Adriana Cardona-Maguigad |  WBEZ

entries.title: Monday's warm temps short-lived, 'severe' storms and 40s expected for Tuesday
entries.summary: <p>Monday’s sunny skies and warm temperatures will likely be record breaking, according to the National Weather Service.</p><p>The predicted high for Chicago is 73 degrees. The last record high for March 9 was 69 degrees in 2014, said Mark Ratzer, a meteorologist with the weather service.</p><p>The high for March 9 is typically about 44 degrees, Ratzer said.</p><p>But the warm weather is short lived. The Chicago area will return to “typical March” weather for the rest of the week, he said.</p><p>Temperatures drop to the 40s on Tuesday with a "severe" storm likely rolling in during the late afternoon and into the evening. That storm will likely bring rain, hail and strong winds across the Chicago area. The southern part of the metro area could also see tornadoes, Ratzer said.</p><p>By the end of the week, temperatures should slightly rise to the high 40s and low 50s. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: As artificial intelligence PAC supports Jesse Jackson Jr., his campaign welcomes AI in Bobby Rush ad
entries.summary: <p>Former Congressman Jesse Jackson Jr. is welcoming an artificial intelligence boost in a new ad that, through AI, helps former Congressman Bobby Rush speak about why he drafted Jackson to return to the seat he held for 17 years.</p><p>The new <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kkhuS9_xnUA" target="_blank" ><u>two-minute digital ad</u></a> features Rush, whose vocal cords were damaged during a battle with cancer, calling Jackson a “lifelong social justice warrior and a passionate advocate for the marginalized and the forgotten." It’s a not so subtle nod of AI approval for Jackson, who is seeing support from the the Leading the Future PAC — funded by OpenAI stakeholders.</p><p>Leading the Future’s top funder is the venture capital firm Andreesen Horowitz. Founder Marc Andreessen is a Trump adviser on tech and business policy — and he gave more than $4.7 million to advance the MAGA agenda in the 2023 and 2024 electoral cycle. Another founding backer is Joe Lonsdale, co-founder of Palantir — an ICE contractor that’s helping the Trump administration deport people.</p><p>Leading the Future had $39 million cash on hand at the end of December, according to the Federal Election Commission, and it is helping to boost both Republicans and Democrats who are AI-friendly. The group also ran ads in Texas's 10th Congressional District and New York's 12th Congressional District. The group uses the super PAC American Mission to support Republicans and Think Big to support Democrats. The PACs are not directly affiliated with the campaigns they're supporting.</p><p>Jackson’s campaign said the Rush-AI ad was discussed prior to the super PAC supporting him — and he supports AI because he wants to connect the district to the global economy.</p><p>“His feeling very strongly is that so many communities he represents are undeserved, marginalized communities. They cannot miss another key economic shift, and he wants his district to be very first in AI,” Jackson campaign spokesman John Digles said. “He wants trade schools to be teaching it. He wants hospitals to have it.”</p><p>Think Big is supporting Jackson with positive ads because he doesn't have a track record of opposing AI, and he has publicly supported it. State Sens. Robert Peters and Willie Preston, both candidates in the 2nd Congressional District race, have voted to regulate AI. Peters is also the co-chair of the state's Generative AI and Natural Language Processing Task Force, a bipartisan effort aimed at exploring the risks and opportunities that AI might bring to the state. </p><p>Cook County Commissioner Donna Miller, also running in the 2nd, supports pursuing AI to ensure the country remains "ahead of the curve," but also says the country must develop a high standard of AI ethics. Her platform includes support for "thoughtful federal regulation." The Cook County Board hasn’t voted to regulate AI.</p><p>Think Big is also running ads to support former Rep. Melissa Bean in her 8th Congressional District primary race.</p><p>There isn’t a singular AI bill on the federal level, but President Donald Trump in December issued an <a class="Link" href="https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2025/12/eliminating-state-law-obstruction-of-national-artificial-intelligence-policy/" target="_blank" ><u>executive order</u></a> that pushes for a “minimally burdensome national standard — not 50 discordant State ones.” </p><p>Trump said he wants a framework that ensures “children are protected, censorship is prevented, copyrights are respected, and communities are safeguarded.” And he accused former President Joe Biden of attempting to “paralyze” the industry. Biden favored an oversight plan, while Trump wants to see deregulation of the industry. Trump immediately rescinded a Biden AI executive order when he took office in January 2025. </p><p>On the state level, Illinois has focused on regulation. Last year, Gov. JB Pritzker signed the Wellness and Oversight for Psychological Resources Act, which limited the use of AI in therapy and psychotherapy services. It also approved the use of AI for licensed behavior health professionals for administrative purposes. Another state law that took effect in January requires the State Board of Education to develop guidance on the use of AI in elementary and secondary education. </p><p>Beyond the battle for AI, the 2nd Congressional District is also being hit hard by Fairshake, a pro-crypto PAC, also funded primarily by Andreessen and Ben Horowitz and targeting Peters in the race. Fairshake is the third Trump-affiliated super PAC to play a role in the race, along with the American Israeli Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC), which is supporting Miller in the race.</p><p>Under attack from all sides, Peters has said <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/02/23/super-pac-influence-congressional-primary-races-illinois" target="_blank" >the three PACs</a> “are scared of having an effective progressive champion in this seat.”</p><p>Rounding out <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/02/24/2nd-congressional-district-march-democratic-primary-robin-kelly-seat" target="_blank" >the field of 10 Democrats</a> vying for the seat left open by Rep. Robin Kelly are Metropolitan Water Reclamation District Board member Yumeka Brown; Adal Regis, who worked in Kelly’s district office; management consultant Eric France; attorney Patrick “PJK” Keating; Toni C. Brown and Sidney Moore.</p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: NFL finalizes compensatory draft picks: Bears get none for Falcons hiring Ian Cunningham
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> did not receive compensatory draft picks for the departure of executive Ian Cunningham to become general manager of the Falcons, even after both teams advocated that the NFL should award them.</p><p>The league released its final list of compensatory picks for the upcoming draft and gave 33 of them to 15 teams, leaving the Bears out.</p><p>As part of the NFL’s push for diversity, a team receives two third-round picks if a minority staff member is hired by another team as general manager. However, the league considers Falcons president of football to be the organization’s “primary football executive,” not Cunningham.</p><p>Ryan has said Cunningham is the decision maker, and Cunningham said last month at the NFL Scouting Combine <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/02/24/bears-falcons-agree-that-bears-should-receive-compensatory-3rd-round-draft-picks-for-ian-cunningham-leaving" >the Bears should receive the picks</a>, which would have been a third-rounder this year and next.</p><p>“It was always my interpretation that if a general manager gets hired, that team would receive two third-round picks,” he said. “I’m the general manager. I was hired. I would think that they would get two third-round picks.</p><p>“I wouldn’t be sitting here if it weren’t for them giving me that job and helping me grow to get this job.”</p><p>That does seem incredibly straightforward.</p><p>Cunningham was the Bears’ assistant general manager under Ryan Poles from 2022 until taking the Falcons job in January. The Bears promoted Jeff King to replace him.</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Traffic diverted after report of shots fired on Eisenhower
entries.summary: <p>Eastbound traffic on Interstate 290 is being diverted from the expressway after a shooting was reported on the roadway, state police said.</p><p>Illinois State Police responded to I-290 eastbound near Austin Avenue, where there was a report of shots fired around 1:50 p.m. No injuries had been reported as of Monday afternoon.</p><p>Traffic is being diverted onto Harlem Avenue as police investigate, and the Harlem ramp is shut down for canvassing, according to Illinois State Police.</p><p>Troopers are asking the public to contact 847-294-4400 or ISP.CrimeTips@illinois.gov, if they witnessed or have information about the shooting. Anyone who was in the area and may have dash-cam footage of the incident is also asked to contact police.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Bulls' Matas Buzelis old enough to attend block party and has VIP pass
entries.summary: <p>SACRAMENTO, Calif. – Sometimes the 21-year-old still comes out.</p><p>It’s not purposeful by any means, and Matas Buzelis has acknowledged that Bulls coach Billy Donovan has pushed him to step up and start taking a veteran leadership role, but there’s still going to be moments when the second-year forward is simply acting his age.</p><p>That happened after Sunday’s loss to the lowly Kings.</p><p>Buzelis was notified by a reporter that he was closing in on 100 blocks for the season and asked what that meant for him.</p><p>Never one to publicly get caught up in his own statistics, he responded, “I don’t really know. I mean 100 is obviously cool, but I don’t really know.”</p><p>A good answer but one that showed he clearly didn’t know the magnitude of that number.</p><p>When then informed that would rank him 7<sup>th</sup> in the league, Buzelis said, “Oh yeah? That’s pretty cool.”</p><p>It sure was, especially for a Bulls roster that has been screaming for consistent rim protection for years. The last Bulls player with 100-plus blocks was Robin Lopez in the 2016-17 season. He finished ranked 10<sup>th</sup> in the league that year.</p><p>Buzelis led the Bulls with 75 blocks as a rookie last season, and the year before that their best shot blocker was guard Alex Caruso with 70.</p><p>Buzelis will enter the Tuesday game against Golden State with 94 blocks in the 2025-26 campaign, averaging 1.5 per game. He will likely hit the 100 mark by the end of the current five-game road trip.</p><p>“I’m really good at timing the block and knowing when guys will try and finish when they’re going up,” Buzelis said of his skillset. “It’s something I feel like I’ve had since I started playing basketball. I just try and translate it to the NBA. Obviously, it’s a little harder, but you got to realize what guys do when they’re driving, how they finish, but I feel like I’ve got the timing and the verticality. Straight up at the rim is hard to finish over me since I’m so tall, but it’s definitely something I have.”</p><p>And something the Bulls need.</p><p>In an offense that is predicated on up-tempo and high pace, blocking a shot and then getting the defense on its heels by pushing the ball back up the floor checks a lot of boxes in what coach Billy Donovan wants to do.</p><p>Factor in that Buzelis is a natural three and can handle the ball, as well as get out in the break, and that adds another dimension to his shot-blocking ability. An ability that Buzelis knows he’s just scratching the surface on.</p><p>Because he considers himself a student of the game, he’s often watching film after games and trying to figure out tendencies of opposing players. He credits that work as another reason why his shot blocking continues to improve.</p><p>“My rookie year I started getting three (blocks in a game) and I was, ‘Alright, I can try and impact the game in that way,’ “ Buzelis said. “Moving forward and guarding players on the perimeter and seeing their tendencies is something I’ve been working on.”</p><p>What Buzelis was also working on as far as the rest of this trip was concerned? Playing with more urgency. Winning is still priority No. 1 for Buzelis and the loss to the Kings was not sitting well.</p><p>“We lost a lot of 50-50 (balls against the Kings), offensive rebounds, in transition, those are things you can fix, you know?” he added. “You’re going to miss shots, and whatever, but you got to win the 50-50s. It always comes down to the minute details in winning.</p><p>“I just didn’t think we came out and competed, myself included. We gotta fix that.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Teen girl killed, 3 others injured after stolen car crashed into a tree
entries.summary: <p>A teenage girl was killed and three other teens were injured after a stolen car they were in crashed into a tree being followed by police early Monday morning in Chicago Lawn, police said. </p><p>Around 3:48 a.m., officers observed a Toyota Corolla speeding with no headlights, police said. Officers activated their emergency equipment and attempted to follow the car. But after police lost sight of the car, the vehicle crashed into a tree in the 6900 block of S. Kedzie Ave, officials said. It is unclear how long police followed the car.</p><p>A victim between ages 15 and 17 was taken to Comer Children's Hospital, where she was pronounced dead. </p><p>A 16-year-old girl was taken to Comer Children's Hospital in serious condition with a head injury and a 15-year-old girl was taken to Comer Children's with a broken arm in good condition, police said. A 16-year-old boy was taken to Mt. Sinai Hospital in fair condition. </p><p>The Toyota was later determined to have been reported stolen.</p><p>The Major Accidents Investigation Unit is investigating.<br></p><ul class="wp-block-post-template is-layout-flow wp-container-core-post-template-is-layout-67e07c73 wp-block-post-template-is-layout-flow" style="--uk-inverse: initial; box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0px; padding: 0px; list-style: none; max-width: 100%; margin-block: 0px; color: rgb(0, 0, 0); font-family: -apple-system, "system-ui", "Segoe UI", Roboto, "Helvetica Neue", Arial, "Noto Sans", sans-serif, "Apple Color Emoji", "Segoe UI Emoji", "Segoe UI Symbol", "Noto Color Emoji"; font-size: 16.9193px; font-style: normal; font-variant-ligatures: normal; font-variant-caps: normal; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: normal; orphans: 2; text-align: start; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; widows: 2; word-spacing: 0px; -webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; white-space: normal; background-color: rgb(255, 255, 255); text-decoration-thickness: initial; text-decoration-style: initial; text-decoration-color: initial;" id="rte-73555f90-1bed-11f1-be03-61f5bd38289e"><li></li></ul>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Man involved in NCAA basketball point-shaving scheme — which included DePaul — pleads guilty
entries.summary: <p>One of the so-called fixers in a sprawling betting scheme that allegedly raked in millions of dollars off of big bets on rigged NCAA basketball games pleaded guilty Monday.</p><p>Jalen Smith appeared in federal court in Philadelphia and pleaded guilty to wire fraud and bribery charges, becoming the first of 26 people charged in the scheme to formally do so. It came a week before the start of March Madness, in which bettors will wager billions legally — and illegally — on the 68 college basketball teams in the tournament. </p><p>Smith, of Charlotte, North Carolina, trained and developed local basketball players for professional scouting combines and used those connections with players when he became part of the scheme, according to prosecutors.</p><p>Charges against Smith and 25 others were unsealed in January. Besides the fixers who recruited players and placed bets, the charges targeted 17 former college basketball players and four other players who were active with their college teams this season.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/01/15/former-depaul-players-charged-in-alleged-scheme-to-rig-basketball-games-bulls" >Former DePaul players and ex-Bull Antonio Blakeney charged in scheme to rig games</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>More than a dozen players tried to fix games as recently as last season and some helped recruit other players, federal prosecutors said.</p><p>It is the latest gambling scandal to hit the sports world since a 2018 U.S. Supreme Court decision unleashed a meteoric rise in legal sports betting.</p><p>Smith was active in helping fix games in the 2023-24 and 2024-25 seasons, placing bets and recruiting players with the promise of a big payment in exchange for purposefully underperforming during a game, prosecutors said.</p><p>The fixers would then bet against the players' teams in those games, defrauding sportsbooks and other bettors, authorities said.</p><p>Smith often traveled to meet players to deliver cash payments by hand, prosecutors said. In one case, Smith traveled to Louisiana to arrange the delivery of about $32,000 in cash to two of the players charged in the scheme, prosecutors said.</p><p>Smith also pleaded guilty to a separate weapons charge, stemming from an FBI search of Smith's bedroom at a house in North Carolina last May where agents found a loaded handgun in a hamper underneath some clothing.</p><p>Smith was prohibited from possessing the weapon as a condition of a drug conviction in 2018, and told agents that he had bought it from a man outside a sneaker store in Charlotte, the plea agreement said. Agents later found that it had been reported stolen.</p><p>The point-shaving scheme began with two games in the Chinese Basketball Association in 2023, according to the indictment. Successful there, fixers recruited Smith and two other fixers and moved on to rigging NCAA games, and the last game they fixed was in January 2025, it said.</p><p>Their scheme grew to involve more than 39 players on more than 17 different NCAA Division I men's basketball teams, who then rigged and attempted to rig more than 29 games, prosecutors said.</p><p>They wagered millions of dollars, raking in "substantial proceeds" for themselves, and paid hundreds of thousands of dollars to players in bribes, prosecutors said. Payments to players typically ranged from $10,000 to $30,000 per game, they said.</p><p>Prosecutors named more than 40 schools where games were allegedly targeted by the scheme. Those included DePaul University and Tulane University.</p><p>Rigged games included major conferences and some playoffs, including the first round of the Horizon League championship and the second round of the Southland Conference championship, prosecutors said.</p><p>Players often recruited teammates to cooperate by playing badly, sitting out or keeping the ball away from players who weren't in on the scheme to prevent them from scoring. Sometimes the attempted fix failed, meaning the fixers lost their bets, prosecutors said.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Funcionarios de inmigración detienen a ciudadana por casi 30 horas en el Aeropuerto O’Hare
entries.summary: <p>Una ciudadana estadounidense de Skokie pasó casi 30 horas bajo custodia de funcionarios federales de inmigración después de ser detenida en el Aeropuerto O’Hare tras un viaje de trabajo el jueves, lo que provocó críticas de familiares, amigos y políticos.</p><p>Sundas “Sunny” Naqvi y cinco colegas, incluyendo a otros dos ciudadanos estadounidenses y tres individuos con tarjetas de residente, fueron todos detenidos en el aeropuerto por agentes de inmigración federales debido a su “historial reciente de viajes”, según informó el comisionado del Condado de Cook y amigo de la familia, Kevin Morrison, a los reporteros afuera del centro del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) en Broadview el domingo.</p><p>Naqvi, de 28 años, y los demás fueron llevados a las instalaciones de Broadview donde luego fueron transferidos a un establecimiento en el Condado de Dodge, Wisconsin, en algún momento de la noche del viernes, según Morrison. Fue liberada a eso de las 5 a.m. el sábado después de casi 30 horas, y una mujer le dio un paseo a un hotel donde se reunió con su familia, que siguió la ubicación de su teléfono hasta que se perdió la señal.</p><p>“Todo lo que le dijeron fue que había un historial de viajes algo curioso, pero no tenían causa para detenerla durante esas 30 horas”, comentó Morrison, añadiendo que los agentes federales “mintieron” a las autoridades locales y funcionarios electos al decir que Naqvi nunca fue detenida.</p><p>Los funcionarios de inmigración no respondieron de inmediato a una solicitud de comentarios.</p><p>“Necesitamos transparencia, necesitamos responsabilidad y debemos asegurarnos de que estemos increíblemente conscientes de los pasos que debemos tomar como nación para proteger todos nuestros derechos civiles y libertades civiles”, agregó Morrison. “El hecho de que esto pueda sucederle a cualquier ciudadano estadounidense debería aterrorizar a todos”.</p><p>La hermana de Naqvi, Sarah Afzal, dijo que casi presenta un informe de persona desaparecida cuando no pudo obtener respuestas de la policía local o federal.</p><p>“Estaba justo aquí y luego estaba en Wisconsin”, dijo Afzal. “Fue aterrador”.</p><p>Un número de candidatos que se postulan para el Congreso estuvieron presentes en Broadview para hablar con los medios de comunicación, incluyendo al candidato del Distrito 7, Reed Showalter, y a los candidatos del Distrito 9, el alcalde de Evanston Daniel Biss, Kat Abughazaleh, Bushra Amiwala y la senadora estatal de Illinois Laura Fine.</p><p>“Sólo fueron traídos de regreso a casa después de que sus familias y nuestra comunidad se negaron a rendirse y siguieron pidiendo respuestas”, dijo Abughazaleh. “Esto se va a convertir en la nueva normalidad si no protestamos”.</p><p>Fine calificó las detenciones de “completamente inadmisibles” y enfatizó la importancia de una supervisión continua del Congreso.</p><p>“No estamos obteniendo la historia completa, no estamos obteniendo todos los hechos y depende del Congreso profundizar en lo que está sucediendo para que entendamos, como ciudadanos y residentes, lo que está pasando y que podamos llegar a la verdad”, dijo Fine.</p><p>Amiwala, quien conoce a Naqvi, elogió los esfuerzos de familiares y amigos para buscar la liberación de la mujer.</p><p>“Para que ella tenga una comunidad como la que tiene —que pudo reunir a tantas personas en tan corto tiempo— es un testimonio y un reflejo de ella y su familia”, dijo Amiwala.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i><u>La Voz Chicago</u></i></a></p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: Girl, 13, shot while filming social media videos in South Side park
entries.summary: <p>Good Samaritans carried a 13-year-old girl to a bus stop after someone opened fire into a park where she was filming social media videos and hit her Friday night in the Oakland neighborhood, police said. </p><p>Officers responded to a call about a person shot in the 3800 block of South Cottage Grove Avenue about 10 p.m. </p><p>According to a police report, the girl was standing inside Mandrake Park recording social media videos with friends, when an armed assailant entered the park near the basketball courts and shot several times in their direction.</p><p>Witnesses carried the girl, who suffered multiple wounds to a bus stop at the northeast corner of Cottage Grove Avenue and Pershing Road, where an ambulance responded and took her in critical condition to Comer Children's Hospital. </p><p>Witnesses told police that the offender fled in a gray Kia SUV.<br></p>
entries.author: Elleiana Green

entries.title: Former Bears linebacker Tremaine Edmunds gets 3-year, $36 million deal from Giants
entries.summary: <p>After one of the best seasons of his career, former <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> linebacker Tremaine Edmunds got a big contract offer from the Giants. He agreed Monday to a three-year, $36 million deal, sources confirmed to the Sun-Times. It includes $24 million guaranteed.</p><p>The Bears released Edmunds, 27, last week for a dead salary-cap hit of $2.4 million rather than pay him $17.4 million this season. He was terrific for the Bears last season with 112 tackles, a career-high four interceptions and a fumble recovery.</p><p>The Bears <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/09/bears-to-sign-playmaking-veteran-devin-bush-jr-as-they-rebuild-linebacker-corps-source" >agreed to a deal with former Browns linebacker Devin Bush Jr. on Monday</a> to replace him. Bush had 125 tackles, three interceptions, two forced fumbles and two sacks last season. He returned two of those interceptions for touchdowns.</p><p>Edmunds was a first-round pick by the Bills in 2018, then signed a four-year, $72 million deal to jump to the Bears in 2023.</p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Bears to sign playmaking veteran Devin Bush Jr. as they rebuild linebacker corps: source
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> will reset at linebacker after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/05/bears-release-tremaine-edmunds-linebacker-salary-cap-nfl" >releasing Tremaine Edmunds</a> by signing veteran Devin Bush, a source said. Bush agreed to a three-year, $30 million deal Monday and will sign when contracts can be finalized Wednesday.</p><p>Bush, 27, is a former No. 10 overall pick who played the last two seasons for the Browns. He started every game last season and finished with 125 tackles, three interceptions, two forced fumbles and two sacks. He returned two of those interceptions for touchdowns.</p><p>In his seven-year career, he has played 98 of a possible 117 games. Most of his missed time was when he tore his ACL in 2020.</p><p>The Steelers drafted Bush out of Michigan in the first round in 2019, and he played four seasons for them before leaving for the Seahawks in free agency. After one season in Seattle, he went to the Browns.</p><p>Bush's father, also named Devin, played in the NFL from 1995 through 2002 and was a starter on the Rams' 1999 championship team.</p><p>Edmunds was with the Bears from 2022 through '24, and the team released him last week to save salary-cap space. He remains a free agent.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a
