entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Chicago carece de casi 225,000 apartamentos asequibles para sus residentes más pobres, según informe
entries.summary: <p>La escasez de vivienda asequible en Chicago está ejerciendo una fuerte presión financiera sobre los inquilinos de bajos ingresos, según un nuevo informe publicado por Housing Action Illinois y la Coalición Nacional de Vivienda de Bajos Ingresos.</p><p>El estudio encontró que sólo hay 31 viviendas asequibles disponibles para alquilar por cada 100 hogares de inquilinos de ingresos extremadamente bajos en Chicago. En Illinois, hay 34 viviendas disponibles por cada 100 hogares inquilinos de ingresos extremadamente bajos.</p><p>Los inquilinos de ingresos extremadamente bajos son aquellos que tienen ingresos anuales en o por debajo del 30% del ingreso promedio del área. En Chicago, eso es $35,970 para una familia de cuatro y $25,200 para un individuo. La mayoría de los hogares considerados de ingresos extremadamente bajos lo son porque tienen trabajos de bajos sueldos, son mayores o tienen una discapacidad, según el informe.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b80000" name="module-b80000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>El <a class="Link" href="file:///Users/s.h./Downloads/gap-report_2026_english.pdf" target="_blank" >informe</a> anual estima la disponibilidad de viviendas asequibles para inquilinos en diferentes niveles de ingresos, con un enfoque en los hogares de ingresos extremadamente bajos. También proporciona estimaciones sobre las necesidades de vivienda asequible para Estados Unidos y las 50 áreas metropolitanas más grandes, incluida Chicago. Se basa en datos de la Encuesta de Comunidades Estadounidenses 2024, una encuesta nacional de aproximadamente 3.5 millones de direcciones.</p><p>Los inquilinos de ingresos extremadamente bajos son los que probablemente se vean más afectados por la escasez de vivienda. Y esto es especialmente cierto en Chicago, dijo Bob Palmer, director de políticas de Housing Action Illinois.</p><p>Dijo que las cifras de Chicago no varían mucho comparado al año pasado, pero los datos muestran que la falta de vivienda asequible en la región es persistente, especialmente para los inquilinos de los ingresos más bajos.</p><p>“Es un problema crónico que todos conocen, pero no ha habido la voluntad política de abordarlo de una manera que cambie fundamentalmente los problemas actuales”, dijo Palmer.</p><p>El informe no encontró ninguna gran ciudad o estado con un número adecuado de viviendas asequibles disponibles para inquilinos de ingresos extremadamente bajos. Y Chicago, que generalmente se considera más asequible que sus contrapartes costeras, no fue la excepción.</p><p>Housing Action Illinois y la Coalición Nacional de Vivienda de Bajos Ingresos encontraron que el área metropolitana de Chicago tiene una escasez de 224,445 unidades de vivienda asequible para sus residentes más pobres. Esto significa que el 76% de los inquilinos de ingresos extremadamente bajos en Chicago están gastando más de la mitad de su ingreso familiar en renta. Los expertos dicen que los inquilinos deben gastar típicamente no más del 30% de su ingreso mensual en vivienda para poder pagar otras necesidades, como atención médica y alimentos.</p><p>“Tienes vecindarios que están gentrificando, donde hay mucha presión sobre la vivienda”, dijo Palmer. “Tienes vecindarios, principalmente en el lado sur u oeste, donde hay una disminución en la existencia de vivienda debido a la desinversión en la vivienda y la demolición de esta”.</p><p>La falta de vivienda asequible contribuye a la falta de hogar y los desalojos, dijo Palmer, así como a la pérdida de población. La población de Illinois ha estado creciendo en los últimos tres años, según el Illinois Policy Institute, pero estaba en una disminución de nueve años antes de eso.</p><p>El crecimiento del estado en los últimos años se ha atribuido en gran medida a un aumento en la inmigración. Sin la inmigración, la población de Illinois habría disminuido, según el instituto. Más de 40,000 residentes dejaron Illinois el año pasado para vivir en otro estado.</p><p>“La vivienda es un problema o un factor que influye muchísimo en el futuro de nuestro estado”, agregó Palmer.</p><p>La asequibilidad de la vivienda sigue siendo un problema debido a las limitaciones en el mercado privado. Gran parte de la construcción reciente de apartamentos en Chicago se considera de lujo o de alta gama porque los constructores no pueden permitirse construir unidades a tasas más bajas sin subsidios gubernamentales, señaló Palmer.</p><p>El informe también reveló que Illinois necesita casi 290,000 casas más para hogares de ingresos extremadamente bajos. Ampliar el acceso a la asistencia de la renta y construir viviendas profundamente asequibles podría ayudar a aliviar la escasez, según la Coalición Nacional de Vivienda de Bajos Ingresos.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Judge deals blow to owner of Cicero diner, won't reverse revocation of video gambling license
entries.summary: <p>More than seven months after the Illinois Gaming Board revoked a license that had allowed Jeffrey Bertucci’s Cicero diner to operate video gambling devices and pay out winnings, he was dealt another blow to his potential cash flow.</p><p>Cook County Judge David B. Atkins ruled Friday that the gaming board had been within its rights to strip away the license from Bertucci’s Firebird Enterprises, Inc., operator of a 24-hour Steak N Egger franchise at 5647 W. Ogden Ave. in the near western suburb.</p><p>The ruling tanked Firebird’s legal efforts to reverse the July 31 revocation, at least for now, though Bertucci can appeal.</p><p>His lawyers had no immediate comment.</p><p>A spokeswoman for the gaming board said the state government agency, which ultimately reports to Gov. JB Pritzker, “is pleased with the Circuit Court of Cook County’s ruling which reaffirms the IGB’s authority to ensure the integrity and safety of gaming as well as those who operate within it.”</p><p> In 2023 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/5/12/23718995/illinois-gaming-board-jeffrey-bertucci-video-poker-cicero-steaknegger-sarno-gambling-machines" target="_blank" >the Chicago Sun-Times discovered</a> the gaming board had licensed Firebird in 2019 nine years after Bertucci testified under a grant of immunity at a federal racketeering trial that targeted reputed mobsters and members of the notorious Outlaws Motorcycle Club.</p><p>Bertucci admitted on the stand that, before video gambling was legalized in Illinois, he’d been involved in a mobbed-up video gambling operation that illegally paid out winnings to patrons.</p><p>That could have been enough to deny him a video gambling license when he applied later, as one of the gaming board’s main tasks is to ensure the integrity of the industry and keep out even the hint of mob influence.</p><p>But regulators licensed him anyway.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e30000" name="image-e30000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f4dc141/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1250x702+0+3/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb6%2Fb9%2F198d93cc4b3697ae90dcb794960d%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-09-at-11-14-10-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Steak N Egger diner in Cicero run by Jeffrey Bertucci." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f4dc141/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1250x702+0+3/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb6%2Fb9%2F198d93cc4b3697ae90dcb794960d%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-09-at-11-14-10-am.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/02f177f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1250x702+0+3/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb6%2Fb9%2F198d93cc4b3697ae90dcb794960d%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-09-at-11-14-10-am.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f4dc141/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1250x702+0+3/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb6%2Fb9%2F198d93cc4b3697ae90dcb794960d%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-09-at-11-14-10-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Steak N Egger diner in Cicero run by Jeffrey Bertucci.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Robert Herguth / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>When the Sun-Times began asking questions in 2023, regulators said they hadn’t known about Bertucci’s testimony, even though it was reported in 2010 in the media.</p><p>The gaming board <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/7/29/23811373/jeffrey-bertucci-illinois-gaming-board-steak-n-egger-cicero-chicago-outfit" target="_blank" >quickly proposed pulling Firebird’s license</a>, but Bertucci appealed, setting a two-year administrative process in motion — during which he was allowed to keep operating his video gambling devices and rake in money.</p><p>More than $200,000 in “net terminal income” was collected during that two-year period.</p><p>Last year, an administrative judge sided with Firebird, saying that had Bertucci come clean about the full extent of his illicit gambling actions when he initially applied for a video gambing license, the gaming board would’ve awarded it anyway.</p><p>The gaming board pushed back on that point and pushed ahead on the revocation — which meant Bertucci’s machines were soon turned off.</p><p>Among other problems, Bertucci initially “lied to federal agents and the grand jury during the federal investigation” he later testified for, the gaming board wrote in its revocation order.</p><p>He also “partnered with known members of organized crime to engage in illegal gambling.”</p><p>Given all this, “we conclude that Respondent could not have demonstrated its suitability for licensure had these facts been known” at the time of licensing, agency officials said.</p><p>In awarding a license, the gaming board has to consider whether an applicant’s “background, including criminal record, reputation and associations, is not injurious to the public health, safety, morals, good order and general welfare of the people of the State of Illinois.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/08/05/cicero-video-gaming-poker-steak-n-egger-jeffrey-bertucci-reputed-organized-crime-illinois-gaming-board" target="_blank" >Firebird sued</a>, saying the gaming board “was aware” of Bertucci’s past “when it granted Plaintiff a license.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-720000" name="image-720000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eb4b61f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1472x826+0+253/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F24%2F01%2F15e453c4445aaf530eeaa0850aa5%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-09-at-11-18-33-am.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3dd00be/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1472x826+0+253/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F24%2F01%2F15e453c4445aaf530eeaa0850aa5%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-09-at-11-18-33-am.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/51f5b8d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1472x826+0+253/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F24%2F01%2F15e453c4445aaf530eeaa0850aa5%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-09-at-11-18-33-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Cook County Judge David B. Atkins." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/51f5b8d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1472x826+0+253/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F24%2F01%2F15e453c4445aaf530eeaa0850aa5%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-09-at-11-18-33-am.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/af501ef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1472x826+0+253/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F24%2F01%2F15e453c4445aaf530eeaa0850aa5%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-09-at-11-18-33-am.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/51f5b8d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1472x826+0+253/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F24%2F01%2F15e453c4445aaf530eeaa0850aa5%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-09-at-11-18-33-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Cook County Judge David B. Atkins.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Illinois Judicial Council</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The agency’s decision to revoke the license “was erroneous and contrary to law because” the gaming board, also known as the IGB, “granted Plaintiff’s establishment license in January of 2019 and repeatedly renewed Plaintiff’s license in January of 2020, 2021, 2022, and 2023 even after the IGB was demonstrably aware of the very facts it claims not to have known when the license was first granted.”</p><p>Firebird’s lawsuit also asserted Bertucci was never “convicted of a crime related to illegal gambling or Grey Games,” the latter a term sometimes used to describe illicit payouts from gaming terminals in bars and restaurants.</p><p>That’s important, Bertucci’s suit says, because as the administrative judge earlier observed, “the Legislature had chosen to limit” the gaming board’s “discretion to deny licensure to applicants who ‘facilitated, enabled, or participated in the use of’ Grey Games by narrowly defining ‘facilitated, enabled, or participated in the use of’ to mean <b>actually convicted</b> [emphasis from lawsuit] of illegal gambling.”</p><p>Atkins ultimately rejected Firebird’s arguments and wrote in his order:</p><p>“It is well settled that findings of fact by an administrative agency are considered to be prima facie true and correct, and courts will not interfere with the discretionary authority of administrative bodies unless the administrative decision is against the manifest weight of the evidence, or is exercised in an arbitrary and capricious manner." </p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/05/01/ballys-casino-license-illinois-gaming-board-reputed-mob-rosemont-difronzo" target="_blank" >Bally’s halts Chicago casino construction over questions about waste hauler dogged by mob allegations</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Robert Herguth

entries.title: Bears bringing back LT Braxton Jones
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> didn't have to look far to find a potential short-term fix at left tackle.</p><p>Now Braxton Jones has to fare better than he did last year.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/08/bears-transaction-tracker-2026-offseason-free-agency-coming-going-ryan-poles-ben-johnson" target="_blank" >Bears</a> are betting he will after agreeing to sign him to a one-year, $5 million contract that could be worth as much as $10 million in 2026, a source confirmed. A full offseason might do the trick — Jones spent all last season recovering from a broken right leg and was unable to add bulk or flexibility. </p><p>Coach Ben Johnson made Jones his reluctant starting left tackle to begin the season, but he was replaced during their Week 4 game against the Raiders and didn't start again. He hurt his knee in Week 7 and was activated from injured reserve before the playoff game against the Rams. He was inactive for that game.</p><p>The Bears were in the market for at least a temporary answer at left tackle because Ozzy Trapilo, who hurt his patellar tendon in the playoff game against the Packers, isn't expected to return until late in the season. The addition of Jones doesn't close the door to the possibility the Bears add depth, likely in the draft, to join Jones and Theo Benedet at the position.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: White Sox cut three players, still have 52 in camp
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — It’s that time of the spring, and the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >White Sox</a> made three more roster cuts before their Cactus League game Monday against the Rockies at Camelback Ranch.</p><p>Right-handers <b>Wikelman Gonzalez </b>and <b>David Sandlin </b>and infielder <b>Tanner Murray </b>were sent to Triple-A Charlotte.</p><p>With the moves, the Sox have 52 players left in camp: 26 pitchers, five catchers, 10 infielders and 11 outfielders.</p><p>‘‘A little bit of a different story with each of them,’’ manager <b>Will Venable </b>explained. ‘‘They all did well and had really productive camps.’’</p><p>About Murray, Venable said: ‘‘I really liked what he did. Really good defensive versatility. He put some charges into some balls. He’s somebody we still value and think he can come help us. But we’ve got other guys ahead of him right now.’’</p><p>About Gonzalez, he said: ‘‘Very much still a work in progress. We really like him, too. He’s such a hard worker and a great guy in the clubhouse. He’s got to throw more strikes and be more aggressive in the zone. He knows that, and he’s working very hard on it.’’</p><p>About Sandlin, he said: ‘‘By the time he got in here, he’d been behind the eight-ball a little bit as far as his build-up. So we want to just make sure what he needs to do to continue build up. And we expect him to be an option at some point, as well. The back was a problem when he got here, and more recently there’s been some elbow soreness. We’re really just being cautious with him.’’</p><p>Gonzales left a game last week because of back spasms, but he worked out on the back fields Monday. Venable said that had nothing to do with his demotion.</p><h3>Happy with Kelenic</h3><p>Venable said he feels as though outfielder <b>Jarred Kelenic </b>is starting to come around.</p><p>Kelenic is in camp on a minor-league contract and must be added to the 40-man roster if he makes the team. A former top prospect for the Mets and Mariners, Kelenic has hit home runs in his last two games after a rough start at the plate. He is hitting .267 this spring after hitting .167 in 65 games last season for the Braves.</p><p>‘‘He’s really been good,’’ Venable said. ‘‘The things we’ve highlighted with him — his athleticism, his defensive ability, his pop at the plate — it’s all coming up now. It’s really about the way he’s just gone about his business, staying on line and staying focused on things that matter.’’</p><p>With the Sox having lost a modern-day record 121 games in 2024 and 102 last season, there’s obviously some room for Kelenic and a lot of other players.</p><p>‘‘If he keeps doing what he’s doing, it’s going to force us to make some tough decisions here,’’ Venable said. ‘‘And that’s what we want. We’ve given these guys transparency that there was a path for a lot of them to make the team. They’re going to make it hard for us, and that’s what we want. That means we’re in a good spot when we have these tough decisions to make.’’</p><h3>Sox 12, Rockies 3</h3><p><b>Everson Pereira </b>played his first spring game Monday and went three innings in right field, as planned, Sox manager <b>Will Venable </b>said. He took six at-bats in an intrasquad game and walked in his only plate appearance against the Rockies.</p><p>‘‘For him, it’s about health right now,’’ Venable said. ‘‘We just need to get him healthy. We just need to get him built up.’’</p><p>Pereira suffered an injury to his right oblique while taking swings off a hitting machine earlier in camp.</p><p>• Infielder <b>Oliver Dunn </b>hit a home run in the second inning against Rockies starter <b>Kyle Freeland</b>. He also led off the Sox’ 11-run fourth with a triple. He finished 3-for-4 and missed the cycle by a double.</p><p>• Attendance was 2,767, but that wasn’t the lowest of the spring for the Sox. The worst was 2,400 on Feb. 25 against the Reds. They’re averaging 3,354 fans for nine home dates.</p><p>• <b>On deck: </b>Sox at Athletics, 3:05 p.m. Tuesday, Mesa, <b>Shane Smith </b>vs. <b>Jeffery Springs.</b></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" name="list-4a0005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, pagination=[{body=[Next Page], href=?p=2, _template=/link/Link.hbs, contentId=null, pianoSectionSlug=null, target=null}], footerBgColor=#000000, pullQuoteIcon=[{image=null, narrowImage=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, alt=null}], bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, stickyHeader=true, listTitleColor=#000000, tableCellBorderColor=#ccd1d5, relatedListFont=font-1, hideAuthors=true, tertiaryTextColor=#6c6c6c, buttonHoverTextColor=#ffffff, tableFont=font-1, items=[{timestamp=1773096136000, title=White Sox assistant pitching coach Bobby Hearn has unique view of former college teammate Shane Smith, type=Image, _styledTemplate=null, imageAlignment=null, publishDate=March 09, 2026 05:42 PM, categoryUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox, isFeatured=false, category=[{items=[White Sox]}], target=null, primaryMediaType=null, url=https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/09/sox-assistant-pitching-coach-bobby-hearn-has-known-shane-smith-since-their-time-together-at-wake-forest, authors=[{authorPageUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/barry-m-bloom, ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, jobTitle=[], _template=/author/byline/AuthorByline.hbs, bio=[], name=[{items=[Barry M. entries.author: Barry M. Bloom

entries.title: White Sox assistant pitching coach Bobby Hearn has unique view of former college teammate Shane Smith
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — Bobby Hearn, as an assistant <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> pitching coach and briefly a teammate to Shane Smith at Wake Forest, has a unique perspective about what’s going on with the staff and the right-hander who’s been tabbed as the Opening Day starter at Milwaukee on March 26.</p><p>Smith came out of high school in Massachusetts with a right-shoulder injury and had to survive that and an elbow problem during his years at Wake Forest. Consequently, the Brewers signed him as an undrafted free agent in 2021, beginning his Major League Baseball journey.</p><p>“Since I graduated from high school my baseball career has never been the same,” Smith said Monday after the announcement. “I’ve never been on any prospect lists. I’ve never been a Top 30 prospect for any team.”</p><p>On Tuesday prior to the Sox playing the Rockies in a Cactus League game at Camelback Ranch, Hearn explained the dynamics of Smith’s statement.</p><p>Hearn, at 29, is every bit as young as he looks.</p><p>“If you’re not a good player you have to coach early,” Hearn quipped.</p><p>But really, then.</p><p>“We both definitely had our ups and downs in college,” Hearn said about his relationship with Smith. “He had a pretty tough couple of years because of injuries starting with the shoulder and an elbow after that. Some tough moments for him. It’s really cool to see him persevere through that.”</p><p>Milwaukee, though, determined Smith had enough stuff when they signed him.</p><p>“When he was at Wake Forest and he stepped on campus you could see the talent,” Hearn said. “He threw hard. Unfortunately, he just couldn’t stay on the field. It was just one of those opportunities where Milwaukee saw something and decided to take a shot on him.”</p><p>But not enough of a shot to keep him in the organization. After three seasons in their minor league system, the Brewers left Smith unprotected and the Sox selected him in the 2024 Rule 5 draft. That comes with some strings attached.</p><p>The cost to the drafting team is $100,000 for the pick, which must remain in the league for the entire following season. In Smith’s case, that was 2025 for the Sox. If not, he must be offered back to the originating team at $50,000. Alternatively, the team could arrange a player swap to keep him.</p><p>Smith excelled, winning seven of his first 10 decisions and earning the nod as the Sox' sole selection to the 2025 American League All-Star squad. He was off to the races and Hearn arrived this past season aa an assistant under new pitching coach Zach Bove.</p><p>“We stayed in touch, relatively,” Hearns said. “Obviously now we’re in day-to-day communication. There are a lot of similarities to what he did in college, which has been funny, but also cool to see. His preparation is unmatched. He took that kind of care he was doing daily to another level.”</p><p>Of course, Smith is not Hearn’s only pitcher to cultivate in his new role with the team. The Sox have yet to designate the remainder of the starting rotation behind Smith, although Davis Martin, Anthony Kay, Sean Burke and Sean Newcoomb seem to be the obvious candidates.</p><p>Hearn found a pliable hierarchy to work under manager Will Venable and Bove. He also found a pitching staff, which is currently still under construction.</p><p>“The culture on the baseball staff has been phenomenal,” Hearn said. “I’ve had a relationship with Zach, but Will has made it incredibly easy to join the group and be yourself. We’ve had a lot of ideas bouncing off the walls. Everyone has a voice. Everyone has an opinion. It’s a great group.”</p><p>About molding the staff, he added: “There’s a ton of talent here. There are a lot of young guys continuing to establish themselves. You want them to feel comfortable. You want them to feel confident, but you need to be very direct with them about what they need to do to get better. I’ve been pretty impressed with their ability to navigate and manage that.”</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.title: Jennifer Runyon Corman, Chicago-born actress with roles in 'Ghostbusters,' 'Charles in Charge,' dead at 65
entries.summary: <p>Jennifer Runyon Corman, an actress born in Chicago who was known for roles in “Ghostbusters” and "Charles in Charge," has died at 65.</p><p>Her representative Scott Ray said she died Friday “surrounded by her family” but didn’t give a cause of death. Her friend "Bewitched" star Erin Murphy posted on <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DVo1paAkdy3/" target="_blank" ><u>Instagram</u></a> that her longtime friend died of cancer.</p><p>"Some people you just know you'll be friends with before you even meet,” Murphy wrote. “She was a special lady."</p><p>In the first season of the TV hit “Charles in Charge,” which premiered in 1984, Runyon Corman played Gwendolyn Pierce, the girlfriend of the title character played by Scott Baio. She briefly reprised her character at the end of Season 2.</p><p>Runyon Corman’s first film credit was in the 1980 thriller "To All a Goodnight."</p><p>Among her other acting credits, she had roles in Season 3 of "The Fall Guy," the 1984 comedy "Up the Creek," Season 7 of "Magnum, P.I.," the premiere episode of the original "Quantum Leap" and "Murder, She Wrote," according to her IMDB page. She also appeared in the 1988 made-for-TV movie "A Very Brady Christmas" as Cindy Brady, the role that Susan Olsen originated in “The Brady Bunch.”</p><p>In one of her biggest roles, in the original “Ghostbusters” movie, Runyon Corman played a student, acting opposite Bill Murray's Dr. Peter Venkman.</p><p>Years later, in an August 2023 <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1aJdEGKD9Y" target="_blank" ><u>interview</u></a> with the Horror Heathen YouTube channel, she said: “When I went to go to the premiere, I was blown away by the movie, and I'm, like, 'Oh, my God. This is a big movie. I'm in a big movie.' It was huge for me. I was so excited but so nervous to meet [Murray]. He just puts you right at ease. It was fun. It was playful."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2b0000" name="image-2b0000"></a> 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/333bf36/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2165x1215+0+13/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2F6b%2F73d40baa4c1ca590979fab7420e2%2Fjennifer-runyon-030826-2-3bdec366bcf14d1c971182d894e9d0a0.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jennifer Runyon in the original “Ghostbusters” movie as a supposedly psychic student being tested by Bill Murray.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Columbia Pictures</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Runyon Corman, who described herself as "an extremely shy child," got her start in Hollywood after moving to California at 12 after the death of her radio DJ father, she said in the interview. Her family's neighbor was a casting director who suggested acting classes to help her "open" up.</p><p>The actress was a pharmacy employee unsure of her career path when a casting director saw her perform a scene in class and offered her an audition for a soap opera.</p><p>Though that gig didn't pan out, she eventually got cast in NBC's "Another World," which brought her to New York. She was one of several actresses who portrayed Sally Frame, a role she played from 1981 to 1983 that helped launch her TV career.</p><p>She is survived by her husband Todd Corman and their two grown children, son Wyatt and daughter Bayley.</p><p>She didn’t act for a decade after her son was born, telling <a class="Link" href="https://www.theladyinredblog.com/the-lady-in-red-blog/archives/07-2016" target="_blank" ><u>The Lady in Red Blog</u></a> in July 2016: “Once we had our son, I kind of knew that I wanted to take some time off just to be a mom. . . . left the business to raise my kids, and we left Hollywood, and we moved to Idaho and Oregon.”</p><p><i>Read more at </i><a class="Link" href="http://usatoday.com" target="_blank" ><i>USA Today</i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: KiMi Robinson | USA Today

entries.title: Fox Lake couple charged with abusing 11-year-old boy
entries.summary: <p>A Fox Lake couple faces felony charges following an investigation into allegations they abused an 11-year-old boy over multiple years.</p><p>Pricilla P. Marshall, 34, and Cody R. Marion, 35, each are charged with felony aggravated battery to a child and misdemeanor counts of domestic battery and endangering the life or health of a child, authorities said Monday.</p><p>According to police, investigators determined the boy had been subjected to abuse by his mother, Marshall, and her boyfriend, identified as Marion, over several years. The boy had been struck with household objects, had dish soap poured into his mouth and ears, and was not permitted to attend school, police allege.</p><p>Police said their investigation began about 9:30 a.m. Friday, when officers responded to the area of Nippersink Boulevard and Grand Avenue for a report of a child found wearing only underwear and appearing to have been battered.</p><p>An employee at a local business reported seeing the boy with blood on his face and multiple welts, bruises, and scratches across his body, police said.<br></p>
entries.author: Daily Herald
